vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Based on the information provided, benzene (CAS 71-43-2) shows a LOW potential to bioaccumulate in humans.

1. Physicochemical indicators
• Molecular weight: 78.1 g mol-1; log Kow ≈ 2.1–2.2 (typical literature value for benzene). 
• These values are below thresholds (log Kow ≥ 3–4) generally associated with high lipid sequestration; therefore intrinsic tendency to bio-concentrate in fat is limited.

2. Absorption and distribution
• Benzene is readily absorbed by inhalation and, to a lesser extent, orally or dermally (ATSDR 2005; EPA 2002 – cited in resources).
• Distribution studies (NTP 1986) indicate temporary presence in blood, liver, lung and—critically—bone marrow, where toxic metabolites are formed, but do not demonstrate long-term storage. 
• EPA National Human Adipose Tissue Survey detected benzene in 96 % of composite adipose samples, but concentrations were very low (n.d.–280 ng g-1 wet weight); this suggests exposure rather than accumulation.

3. Metabolism and elimination
• Benzene is rapidly metabolised by CYP2E1-mediated oxidation to benzene oxide → phenol → hydroquinone, catechol, muconic acid, etc. (Ross 2000; USEPA 1998).  
• The principal elimination pathway is urinary excretion of conjugated metabolites (Sabourin et al. 1989; Sanders et al. 2001).  
• Kinetic data in rodents and limited human volunteer data show biphasic blood elimination with initial half-lives of minutes and terminal half-lives of a few hours; >90 % of absorbed dose is excreted within 24–48 h. 

4. Tissue persistence
• No evidence of progressive accumulation in adipose tissue or other organs after repeated exposure; adipose levels reflect recent exposure rather than long-term storage. 
• Reactive metabolites form covalent adducts in bone marrow, contributing to toxicity, but these adducts represent a toxicodynamic concern rather than true bioaccumulation of the parent compound.

5. Weight-of-evidence conclusion
• Physicochemical data (low log Kow), rapid metabolism, urinary excretion of metabolites, and low biomonitoring levels all indicate minimal potential for long-term build-up.
• Systemic availability after exposure is HIGH (readily absorbed), but persistence in the body is LOW and tissue accumulation is LOW.

Uncertainties / data gaps
• Direct human half-life studies for benzene are limited; most kinetic values come from animal models or mixed-VOC human studies. 
• Bone-marrow-bound metabolites may linger longer than parent benzene, but quantitative human data are scarce.

Overall classification
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (due to marrow-targeting metabolites). 
• Systemic availability: HIGH (efficient absorption).  
• Bioaccumulation / persistence: LOW.

Hence, from a risk-assessment perspective, benzene’s hazard arises from its rapid metabolic activation rather than from bioaccumulation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Bioaccumulation Potential of Arsenite (CAS 15502-74-6; common synonym: sodium arsenite, NaAsO2)

1. Chemical identity and relevance to assessment  
   • CAS 15502-74-6 is an inorganic trivalent arsenic salt (AsIII).  
   • Because the toxicokinetics of inorganic arsenite are driven by the AsIII ion rather than the counter-ion (Na+), data on sodium arsenite and other AsIII salts are regarded as directly applicable (“read-across” within the same ionic species).

2. Systemic availability (Absorption) – HIGH  
   • Oral: Human volunteer studies and accidental-exposure case reports indicate 80–95 % absorption from the gastrointestinal tract (ATSDR 2007; WHO 2001).  
   • Inhalation: Respiratory deposition efficiency 30–60 % for respirable particles; soluble AsIII is rapidly absorbed across the lung epithelium (IARC 2020).  
   • Dermal: Limited but measurable uptake (≤3 %) has been shown in in-vitro human skin studies; not a major route for the general population.

3. Distribution  
   • After absorption, AsIII distributes with blood to highly perfused organs. Peak blood concentrations occur within 1–2 h post-ingestion.  
   • AsIII readily enters cells through aquaglyceroporin channels and binds to sulfhydryl groups of proteins and low-molecular-weight thiols (e.g., glutathione).  
   • Highest short-term soft-tissue concentrations: liver, kidney, lung; longer-term retention: keratin-rich matrices (nails, hair) and, to a lesser extent, bone (ATSDR 2007).

4. Metabolism (biotransformation)  
   • Main pathway: enzymatic methylation (AS3MT) → monomethylarsonic acid (MMA) → dimethylarsinic acid (DMA).  
   • AsIII and MMAIII are considered more cytotoxic and genotoxic than DMA.  
   • Methylation efficiency varies widely between individuals (genetic polymorphisms, nutrition, age), affecting residence time.

5. Excretion  
   • Urine is the dominant route (60–75 % of absorbed dose within 3–4 days). Species pattern in urine: 10–30 % iAs, 10–20 % MMA, 40–70 % DMA.  
   • Biological half-life in blood/plasma: 2–4 days. Whole-body half-life (terminal) estimated at 10–30 days when all compartments—including slowly exchanging hair, nails, and bone—are considered (NRC 2001; EFSA 2009).  
   • Fecal excretion and desquamation of keratin tissues provide minor additional clearance.

6. Evidence for tissue accumulation and persistence  
   • Hair/nails: Concentrations build up in proportion to chronic intake and remain for the life of the keratin filament (≈3–6 months). They are widely used as biomarkers of past exposure.  
   • Soft tissues: After cessation of exposure, >90 % of arsenic is cleared from blood and most organs within weeks. No progressive increase is seen if steady-state exposure is unchanged.  
   • Lipid tissues: Negligible accumulation—compound is inorganic, hydrophilic.  
   • Bone: Low-level incorporation has been observed (0.5–1 % of body burden) with an estimated half-life of several months, but this pool does not continuously increase at environmental exposure levels.

7. Comparison with regulatory bioaccumulation metrics  
   • Conventional bioaccumulation criteria (e.g., BCF ≥5000 for “very bioaccumulative” under REACH) were developed for organic, lipophilic chemicals and are not directly applicable to ionic metals/metalloids.  
   • Nevertheless, kinetic data (rapid urinary clearance, short plasma half-life) indicate that inorganic arsenite does NOT meet criteria for high bioaccumulation in humans.  
   • Biomagnification across trophic levels has not been demonstrated for inorganic arsenic species (WHO 2001).

8. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
   • Systemic availability: HIGH (efficient absorption).  
   • Tissue accumulation:  
     – Soft tissues: LOW (fast clearance).  
     – Keratin tissues (hair, nails): MEDIUM (time-integrated storage, but biologically inert).  
     – Bone: LOW-to-MEDIUM (minor long-term depot).  
   • Overall human bioaccumulation potential of arsenite is classified as LOW-to-MEDIUM. Persistent low-level body burdens can occur with chronic exposure, yet the element does not progressively build up to ever-increasing levels once exposure stops because the majority is eliminated within weeks.

9. Key references (representative)  
   • ATSDR. 2007. Toxicological Profile for Arsenic. U.S. Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry.  
   • WHO. 2001. Environmental Health Criteria 224: Arsenic and Arsenic Compounds (2nd ed.).  
   • IARC. 2020. Arsenic and Arsenic Compounds. IARC Monographs Volume 100C (Update).  
   • EFSA. 2009. Scientific Opinion on Arsenic in Food. EFSA Journal 7(10):1351.  
   • National Research Council (NRC). 2001. Arsenic in Drinking Water: 2001 Update.

10. Uncertainties and data gaps  
   • Inter-individual variability in methylation efficiency can alter residence times by a factor of 2–3.  
   • Co-exposure to selenium, folate status, age, and disease can modify kinetics.  
   • Limited quantitative data exist on very low-level chronic exposures (<1 µg/kg bw/day) and on bone half-life in humans.

Overall assessment: Arsenite exhibits high systemic availability but only limited capacity to bioaccumulate in metabolically active tissues due to efficient biotransformation and urinary excretion. Some medium-term persistence is observed in hair, nails, and to a minor extent in bone, but the compound is not considered highly bioaccumulative in humans.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence from the cited human, primate and rodent kinetic studies indicates that methylmercury (CAS-No. 16056-34-1) has a HIGH bioaccumulation potential in humans.

1. Absorption and initial distribution
• Human dietary absorption from fish is very efficient (>90%).
• Once in blood, methylmercury forms a cysteine complex that mimics methionine; the complex is actively taken up by many tissues, crosses both the blood–brain barrier and the placenta.

2. Tissue accumulation patterns (Sherlock et al., 1984; Vahter et al., 1994)
• Brain: highest long-term repository. In Sherlock et al. adult volunteers, brain concentrations remained ≥1 µg/kg for the entire 800-day observation period and declined only slowly.
• Kidney & liver: reach high levels soon after exposure; elimination is somewhat faster than from brain but still protracted (kidney MM still detectable at 800 days).
• Blood: mirrors recent intake; falls more rapidly than organ tissues but shows a mono-exponential half-life of ≈50–70 days.
• Hair (not graphed here but consistently reported) retains mercury in proportion to blood, providing a biomarker of cumulative exposure.

3. Persistence / biological half-life
• Whole-body terminal half-life in humans ≈ 1 year; blood half-life ≈ 50–70 days (Sherlock et al., 1984).
• Infant monkeys show comparable kinetics (Vahter et al., 1994), supporting inter-species concordance.
• Rodent data (Farris et al., 1993) show faster clearance (≤150 days), suggesting humans are the worst-case species for persistence.

4. Limited metabolic clearance
• Demethylation to inorganic Hg occurs slowly; the inorganic fraction (IM) rises gradually (liver IM, kidney IM curves) but is itself poorly eliminated, further extending residence time.
• Enterohepatic recirculation of conjugated forms also delays excretion.

5. Bioaccumulation classification
• High systemic availability (efficient GI uptake, extensive tissue distribution).
• High toxicodynamic activity (CNS target-organ concentrations associated with Minamata, Iraq outbreaks).
• Therefore methylmercury meets criteria for high bioaccumulative and persistent substances in humans.

6. Uncertainties / modifying factors
• Individual variation in seafood intake, selenium status and genetic differences in demethylation can influence body burden.
• Data gaps on very long-term (>5 year) elimination, but available curves already demonstrate multi-year persistence.

Conclusion: Methylmercury is readily absorbed, widely distributed, and cleared only slowly (half-life ≈ 1 year). It preferentially accumulates in brain, kidney, and liver and can persist for years after exposure ceases, justifying its designation as a chemical of high bioaccumulation and persistence in humans.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Bioaccumulation Potential of Vinyl Chloride (VC; chloroethene; CAS 75-01-4) in Humans  

1. Key Physico-chemical Parameters (PubChem CID 6333; ECHA, 2023)  
   • Molecular weight: 62.5 g mol⁻¹  
   • log Kₒw: 1.2 – 1.6 (25 °C) – low lipophilicity  
   • Water solubility: ≈ 1.1 g L⁻¹ (25 °C)  
   • Henry’s law constant: ≈ 1.1 atm m³ mol⁻¹ – very volatile  
   • Vapour pressure: 2.6 × 10⁵ Pa (25 °C) – high  
   Interpretation: These values favour rapid distribution in blood/air and poor retention in lipids.

2. Absorption and Initial Distribution  
   • Inhalation is the dominant route. Uptake efficiency in humans ≈ 40-60 % of inspired dose at rest (Filser et al., 1992; ATSDR, 2006).  
   • Dermal and oral absorption are minor because the compound partitions into air before contacting skin or gut contents.  
   • After entry, VC equilibrates quickly between blood and highly perfused tissues; however, its low log Kₒw limits affinity for adipose tissue.

3. Metabolic Fate and Elimination  
   • Primary biotransformation: CYP2E1-mediated epoxidation to chloroethylene oxide (CEO) and subsequent rearrangement to chloroacetaldehyde, 2-chloroethanol, and ultimately CO₂ or conjugated urinary metabolites (e.g., thiodiglycolic acid, TDGA).  
   • Exhalation of unchanged VC: 30-60 % of absorbed dose is breathed out within minutes (Bolt et al., 1976).  
   • Blood half-life (humans): 0.3-1 h after cessation of exposure (Monster et al., 1976; EPA IRIS, 2000).  
   • Urinary metabolites are largely eliminated within 24-48 h (IARC, 2012).  
   • No unchanged parent compound is detected in urine or faeces, confirming rapid clearance.

4. Tissue Retention Studies  
   • Autopsy/biopsy data from chronically exposed workers do not show measurable parent VC in fat or liver (Sharma & Loew, 1977).  
   • Rodent studies with continuous inhalation (50-300 ppm) show no progressive rise of parent VC in liver or fat when measured 16 h after exposure (Withey & Collins, 1979).  
   • Radio-label studies (^14C-VC) demonstrate > 95 % of total radioactivity lost as CO₂, volatile VC, or polar metabolites within 72 h (Miller et al., 1978). Small fractions (< 1 %) remain bound to macromolecules (DNA, protein). These adducts are biologically important for carcinogenicity but do not constitute “bioaccumulation” of the parent substance.

5. Biological Half-Life and Persistence  
   • Parent VC: minutes to < 2 h in blood; effectively cleared within a few hours.  
   • Reactive metabolites: short-lived (seconds–minutes) before conjugation.  
   • DNA/protein adducts: can persist for days to weeks but are present at trace levels (pmol mg-DNA⁻¹). They represent toxicodynamic hazard, not storage of bulk chemical.

6. Biomonitoring Indicators  
   • Breath analysis reliably reflects recent (same-shift) exposure.  
   • Urinary TDGA peaks ~10-12 h post-shift and returns to baseline in 1-2 days (Ghittori et al., 2005).  
   • No biomarker indicates long-term body burden of VC itself.

7. Criteria-based Weight-of-Evidence Assessment  
   • Physico-chemical properties: unfavourable for lipid storage (log Kₒw < 2).  
   • Rapid metabolism + exhalation: short biological half-life.  
   • Experimental/occupational data: no progressive tissue build-up.  
   • Regulatory classifications (e.g., ECHA PBT assessment, 2023): VC is not considered bioaccumulative (B-criterion not met).  

   Classification:  
   • Systemic availability (inhalation): HIGH – readily absorbed.  
   • Bioaccumulation potential (tissue build-up of parent compound): LOW.  
   • Persistence of parent compound in body: LOW (hours).  
   • Persistence of covalent adducts: not relevant for bioaccumulation but important for carcinogenicity.

8. Uncertainties and Data Gaps  
   • Limited modern human PBPK studies at very low environmental levels (µg m⁻³).  
   • Data on repeated dermal exposure are scarce, but this route is negligible in realistic scenarios.  
   • While adduct data clarify mechanism of action, the relationship between adduct burden and cancer risk at low doses remains uncertain.

Conclusion  
Vinyl chloride is readily taken up via inhalation but is highly volatile, has low lipophilicity, and is metabolised/exhaled quickly. Weight-of-evidence from human biomonitoring, animal studies, and physicochemical criteria indicates a LOW potential for bioaccumulation of the parent compound in human tissues. Its toxicological concern relates to transient but reactive metabolites, not to persistence or storage of the parent chemical in the body.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Bioaccumulation assessment for  
6PPQ ( = 6PPD-quinone; N-(1,3-dimethyl-butyl)-N´-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-quinone;  
CAS 2754428-18-5)

1. Physico-chemical parameters (measured or modelled)

• Molecular weight (MW) ≈ 300 g mol⁻¹  
• Calculated log Kow (EPI Suite v4.11, QSAR Toolbox v4.6) 5.0 – 5.4  
• Water solubility (25 °C, predicted) ≈ 0.05 – 0.2 mg L⁻¹  
• pKa no dissociable groups in the environmentally relevant range (4–10)  
• Vapour pressure (predicted) ≤ 5 × 10⁻⁶ Pa (non-volatile)

Interpretation  
– Log Kow > 4 suggests potential for lipid partitioning.  
– The quinone moiety adds polarity relative to 6PPD (log Kow ≈ 6.7), lowering lipophilicity and increasing aqueous reactivity.  

2. Experimental information

No direct human kinetic or biomonitoring data are yet available for 6PPQ. Therefore a weight-of-evidence (WoE) is built from:

a) Measurements in aquatic species  
   • EPA ECOTOX data show bioconcentration factors (BCF) of 167–540 L kg⁻¹ (whole fish, < 7 d exposure; Tian et al., 2022).  
   • These BCF values are below the REACH “bioaccumulative” threshold (2 000 L kg⁻¹) and orders of magnitude lower than those for classic persistent, bioaccumulative substances (e.g. PCBs).

b) In-silico human toxicokinetic modelling  
   • pkCSM and ADMET Predictor give a human steady-state volume of distribution (Vss) of 0.8–1.2 L kg⁻¹ (moderate).  
   • Predicted plasma protein binding ~97 %.  
   • Predicted intrinsic hepatic clearance (CLint) is high (70–140 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹), driven by the quinone’s electrophilicity and susceptibility to phase II conjugation (GSH, glucuronide).  
   • Predicted human half-life < 12 h for a single oral dose.

3. Metabolism and elimination (read-across from parent 6PPD and aromatic para-phenylenediamines)

• 6PPD has been shown in rats and humans (Zhang et al., 2021; Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 432:115785) to undergo rapid phase I (N-oxidation, hydroxylation) followed by glucuronidation/sulphation and urinary excretion; the parent compound is non-detectable in blood after 24 h.  
• Quinone derivatives of anilines are even more reactive: they readily form GSH and cysteine conjugates catalysed by GST.  
• The high reactivity shortens systemic half-life but may create covalent protein adducts; such adducts are typically removed by proteolysis, not stored long-term in fat.

4. Distribution to lipid tissues

• Physiologically-based TK modelling (Schmitt et al., 2023, Environ. Sci. Technol. 57:4562–4570) predicts human lipid:blood partition coefficient of ≈ 5 for 6PPQ, compared with > 50 for highly bioaccumulative POPs.  
• For a compound with fast metabolic clearance (CLhep > 1 L h⁻¹) the modelled steady-state adipose concentration stays below 0.1 µM even under constant daily intake of 1 µg kg⁻¹ bw.

5. Persistence in the human body

• Using the clearance estimates above the elimination half-life in humans is expected to be < 1 day for free 6PPQ, somewhat longer for protein adducts (estimated 1–3 days) but far below the half-lives of bioaccumulative organochlorines (years).  
• No evidence of sequestration in bone, hair or other slow-turnover compartments.

6. Comparison with regulatory criteria

REACH and OECD regard BMF> 1 or human half-life ≥ 3 months as indicators of bioaccumulation. All available data and models for 6PPQ fall well below these benchmarks.

7. Uncertainties & data gaps

• Lack of direct human biomonitoring (blood/urine) data.  
• Limited repeat-dose TK studies; most data are acute or short-term.  
• Possible formation of unknown, more persistent metabolites has not been excluded.  
• Protein adduction may cause local-tissue persistence not captured by lipid partitioning models.

8. Weight-of-Evidence conclusion

Toxicodynamic reactivity: Medium–High (electrophilic quinone, can redox-cycle and form adducts).  
Systemic availability: Medium (oral and dermal absorption plausible; good plasma binding).  
Bioaccumulation potential in humans: Low.

Rationale  
Although 6PPQ is moderately lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 5), it is also highly susceptible to metabolic transformation and conjugation. Predicted and measured BCF values are below regulatory concern levels, and modelling indicates short human half-lives with negligible build-up in adipose tissue. Covalent binding to proteins may occur but is transient compared with classic persistent organic pollutants. Overall, 6PPQ is unlikely to accumulate to appreciable levels in human tissues or persist for extended periods once exposure ceases.

Citations (representative)  
• Tian, C. et al. 2022. Environ. Sci. Technol. 56, 4742-4751.  
• Zhang, Y. et al. 2021. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 432:115785.  
• Schmitt, W. et al. 2023. Environ. Sci. Technol. 57:4562-4570.  
• US-EPA EPI Suite v4.11; QSAR Toolbox v4.6 (2023 release).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Bioaccumulation assessment  
Substance: Phenanthrene (synonyms: 9,10-Benzopheneanthrene, Phenantrin; CAS 85-01-8)  
Structure: 3-ring polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH)  
Molecular weight: 178.23 g·mol⁻¹

1. Key physico-chemical drivers  
• Log KOW (25 °C): 4.3–4.6 (ECHA, PubChem) – indicates an intrinsic tendency to partition into lipids.  
• Water solubility: 1–1.6 mg L⁻¹ – low.  
• Vapour pressure: 6–8 × 10⁻⁴ Pa – semi-volatile.  
• pKa: non-ionisable – bioavailability is not pH-limited.  
Taken alone, a log KOW around 4.5 would suggest potential for bioaccumulation; however, metabolic capability must be examined.

2. Absorption  
• Inhalation: Readily absorbed in rodents (Johanson et al., 1988). Human PBPK modelling (Westerink et al., 2010) predicts ~70 % uptake for the vapour.  
• Dermal: In vitro human skin 24-h absorption ≈ 10 % of dose (Wester et al., 1992) – relatively low but not negligible.  
• Oral: No direct human data; rat studies show ~80 % systemic uptake for PAHs of similar MW (Sun et al., 2010).  
Systemic availability: Medium.

3. Distribution  
• Due to lipophilicity, parent phenanthrene partitions initially into blood lipoproteins and fatty tissues.  
• Human autopsy/breast-milk surveys show detectable, but low (ng–µg kg⁻¹ lipid) phenanthrene levels compared with higher-ring PAHs (WHO/UNEP, 2012).  
• Modelling indicates short-term adipose storage with rapid decline once exposure stops.

4. Metabolism (toxicokinetics)  
• Phase I: CYP1A1/1A2 oxidise to phenanthrene-1,2- and ‑3,4-epoxides → dihydrodiols, phenols, quinones.  
• Phase II: Glucuronidation and sulfation.  
• In vitro human liver microsomes t½ for parent ≈ 15–25 min (Jerina et al., 2000).  
• In vivo human biomonitoring: urinary 1-hydroxy-, 3-hydroxy- and phenanthrene tetrols peak 6–12 h after inhalation exposure to cigarette smoke; elimination half-life 6–35 h (Li et al., 2012).  
Rapid and extensive biotransformation constitutes the main depuration route.

5. Excretion  
• Urine: 70–90 % of absorbed dose (mostly conjugated metabolites) within 48 h.  
• Faeces: Minor pathway for human; major in rodents when bile excretion is pronounced.  
• Parent compound rarely detected in urine or blood after 24 h.

6. Empirical and modelling bioaccumulation data  
• Human biomonitoring: adipose phenanthrene generally <1 µg·kg⁻¹ lipid; breast-milk median 0.06 µg·kg⁻¹ lipid (Fischer et al., 2015). These concentrations are markedly lower than for highly persistent organochlorines.  
• PBPK models fitted to firefighter exposure scenarios give effective whole-body biological half-life of 1–2 days (Laitinen et al., 2014).  
• Fish BCF: 1000–3500 L·kg⁻¹ (OECD 305). Meets the REACH “B” threshold for aquatic species, but fish lack the high metabolic capacity found in mammals; hence read-across to humans would over-predict.

7. Weight-of-evidence statement  
• The compound is sufficiently lipophilic to enter fatty tissues (high log KOW).  
• However, it is also readily oxidised, conjugated, and excreted in humans; measured biological half-lives are in the order of hours to a few days, not weeks or months.  
• Biomonitoring consistently demonstrates low body burdens and rapid clearance relative to more persistent PAHs or halogenated organics.

8. Uncertainties and gaps  
• No human controlled dosing studies for phenanthrene per se (ethical constraints).  
• Limited data on repeated high-dose occupational scenarios; current inferences rely on firefighters and smokers.  
• Potential formation of reactive metabolites (tetrols) is recognised, but this affects toxicity rather than bioaccumulation.

9. Conclusion  
Systemic availability: Medium (readily absorbed by inhalation and oral routes, somewhat limited dermally).  
Toxicodynamic activity: Medium (genotoxic metabolites, but not the focus here).  
Bioaccumulation potential in humans: LOW.  
Persistence in human tissues: LOW to MODERATE transient storage; biological half-life ≤ 2 days; no evidence of long-term build-up upon cessation of exposure.

Therefore, although phenanthrene can temporarily distribute to lipid-rich tissues, efficient human metabolism and elimination prevent significant or persistent bioaccumulation.

human metabolism and elimination of phenanthrene

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Bioaccumulation assessment for  
Substance: Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP)  
CAS-No.: 115-86-6  
Synonyms: TPP, Phosphoric acid triphenyl ester, Triphenoxy-phosphate

1. Physico-chemical parameters that determine bioaccumulation

• Molecular weight: 326.3 g mol⁻¹  
• Log Kow: 4.3–4.9 (25 °C; PubChem CID 13527, ECHA dossier)  
• Water solubility: 1–2 mg L⁻¹ (25 °C)  
• Vapour pressure: 2.6 × 10⁻⁵ Pa (25 °C)  
• pKa: does not ionise in the physiological pH range

Interpretation: The log Kow is high enough to suggest some lipophilicity, but it is below the threshold (log Kow ≥ 5.5) usually associated with strong bioaccumulation. In addition, the compound is an aryl‐phosphate tri-ester that is rapidly hydrolysed by esterases, which strongly limits persistence in vivo.

2. ADME and human toxicokinetic data

Absorption  
• Oral: Rat gavage studies (WHO, 1991; Stauber et al., 1987) show ≥80 % absorption.  
• Dermal: In vitro human skin diffusion cells report 7–14 % of applied dose in 24 h (Fasano et al., 2017).  
• Inhalation: No direct human data, but the compound is particle-bound in indoor dust; pulmonary absorption is assumed to be moderate.

Distribution  
• Experimental rat study with 14C-TPHP (Stauber et al., 1987) found transient distribution to liver, kidney and adipose tissue but >70 % of radioactivity had been eliminated within 24 h.  
• Human biomonitoring indicates parent TPHP is rarely detected in blood; the main circulating species is its metabolite diphenyl phosphate (DPHP) (Carignan et al., 2013; Ospina et al., 2018).

Metabolism  
• Rapid oxidative/enzymatic cleavage by para- and carboxyl-esterases to DPHP, mono-phenyl phosphate and phenol.  
• In vitro intrinsic clearance in pooled human liver microsomes: 4.2 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹, indicating high metabolic capacity (van den Eede et al., 2013).  
• PBPK modelling fits urinary DPHP data with biological half-life (t½) of 8–20 h (Schutze et al., 2019).

Excretion  
• Human volunteers exposed via a handwipe-to-dietary pathway excreted 75–90 % of the absorbed dose as urinary DPHP within 24 h (Hammel et al., 2019).  
• Minor biliary/faecal elimination is reported in rats (<10 %).

3. Empirical human and animal indicators of bioaccumulation / persistence

• Blood/serum: Median concentrations <0.2 ng mL⁻¹ in NHANES 2013–2014 (Ospina et al., 2018).  
• Breast milk: <0.5 ng g lipid (Zhang et al., 2017) – orders of magnitude lower than classical POPs.  
• Adipose tissue: No consistent detection in limited surgical biopsy studies (Stapleton et al., 2014).  
• Fish BCF values 37–160 (OECD 305), far below the regulatory bioaccumulative cut-off (BCF = 2 000).  
• Estimated human in-vivo half-life for parent TPHP: <12 h; for DPHP: 12–24 h (Carignan et al., 2013).

4. Weight-of-evidence evaluation

A. Systemic availability  
– Absorption (oral, dermal, inhalation) is appreciable.  
– However, first-pass and systemic hydrolysis are rapid.  
Classification: Medium systemic availability (due to good absorption counter-balanced by fast clearance).

B. Potential for tissue accumulation  
– Log Kow just below strong bioaccumulation range.  
– Extensive Phase-I hydrolysis → polar metabolites that do not partition into lipids.  
– Short biological half-life; steady-state body burdens remain low unless exposure is continuous.  
– Biomonitoring shows low frequency and low magnitude detections in blood, milk, adipose tissue.

C. Persistence in the human body  
– Parent compound: low (half-life hours).  
– Primary metabolite (DPHP): slightly longer but still <1 day.  
– No evidence for metabolite sequestration or non-extractable tissue residues.

Overall bioaccumulation potential in humans: Low.

5. Uncertainties / data gaps

• Very few controlled human kinetic studies; estimates rely on urinary DPHP which could slightly over- or under-predict half-lives.  
• Lack of repeated-dose kinetic data for sensitive sub-populations (pregnant women, neonates).  
• No longitudinal data on low-level continuous exposure (e.g., indoor dust inhalation) spanning months to years; under such conditions a quasi-steady state could arise even with short half-life.

6. Conclusion

Triphenyl phosphate is efficiently absorbed by the oral and, to a lesser extent, dermal and inhalation routes. Nevertheless, rapid enzymatic hydrolysis to water-soluble metabolites leads to fast urinary elimination (biological half-life ≈ 0.5–1 day). Empirical biomonitoring, PBPK modelling, and animal radiolabel studies consistently show only transient presence of the parent compound and low concentrations in lipid-rich tissues. Therefore, TPHP has a LOW potential to bioaccumulate or persist in human tissues under typical environmental or occupational exposure scenarios.

Factors influencing TPHP retention in humans

tests.prompt: Assess the bioaccumulation potential of {chemical_and_CASRN} in human. Discuss its potential for accumulation in tissues and its persistence in the body.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Human data specific to elemental lanthanum (CAS 7439-91-0) are limited, but several lines of evidence can be extracted from the cited literature on lanthanides as a group and on lanthanum-containing workplaces.

1. Absorption and initial systemic availability
   • Repeated‐dose studies in rodents showed gastrointestinal absorption of moderately soluble lanthanides (cerium cited as surrogate) to be <0.1 % in adults, although uptake was higher in neonates (Bulman 2003; HEI 2001).  The same low oral absorption is generally assumed for lanthanum because of its similar ionic radius and insolubility.
   • No quantitative human oral absorption data were located in IUCLID (2000) (explicit data gap).
   • Inhalation can deliver lanthanum directly to the lung: autopsy of a worker exposed 46 years in engraving gave lung levels 2 000-fold and lymph-node levels 50-fold higher than in unexposed controls (HEI 2001).  A printer exposed to carbon-arc lamp emissions also showed elevated pulmonary La (Dufresne et al. 1994).

2. Tissue distribution in humans
   • Teeth: environmental monitoring showed lanthanum is incorporated into human tooth matrix (Arch Oral Biol 49, 2004), confirming a route for long-term deposition.
   • Lungs and lymph nodes show the highest accumulation after decades of occupational exposure (HEI 2001; Dufresne et al. 1994).
   • No quantitative human blood or urine kinetic studies were found (data gap).

3. Persistence / biological half-life
   • Workers’ lungs still contained very high lanthanum burdens at death, indicating biological half-lives extending to years (HEI 2001).  No clearance half-life values were reported, but the magnitude of enrichment after long‐term exposure argues for slow elimination from lung tissue.
   • In rodents, repeated dosing lengthened whole-body retention half-times for lanthanides (HEI 2001), suggesting that once deposited, elimination is slow.  By analogy, lanthanum in human lung or bone is expected to persist for years.

4. Read-across considerations and limitations
   • Most kinetic numbers come from cerium or mixed lanthanide studies.  Read-across is justified by very similar +3 oxidation state, ionic radius and insolubility, but uncertainty remains because solubility and protein binding can differ between individual REEs.
   • No models or measurements were available for soft tissues (liver, kidney) or for intravenously administered lanthanum.

5. Weight-of-evidence conclusion
   • Systemic availability: LOW after oral exposure because of very poor absorption (<0.1 % in rodents, no contradictory human data).  MEDIUM after chronic inhalation because particles bypass the gut and deposit in lung.
   • Bioaccumulation potential:  – In bone/teeth: MEDIUM.  Environmental studies show incorporation but at trace concentrations.  – In lung/lymph nodes of exposed workers: HIGH, as concentrations were orders of magnitude above background after many years, and clearance is slow.
   • Persistence: HIGH in lung and likely bone, suggested by occupational case reports and rodent retention data; specific human half-lives are not available, representing a data gap.

Overall, elemental lanthanum exhibits low systemic bioaccumulation potential following oral exposure but a high potential to accumulate and persist in pulmonary tissue (and to a lesser extent in hard tissues) after long-term inhalation exposure.  Key uncertainties arise from the paucity of quantitative human kinetic data outside of autopsy studies.
searched_keyphrases: Persistence and biological half-life of lanthanum
Renal clearance and excretion of lanthanum

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation – Bioaccumulation Potential of Galaxolide (HHCB; 1,3,4,6,7,8-hexahydro-4,6,6,7,8,8-hexamethyl-cyclopenta-γ-2-benzopyran; CAS 1222-05-5)

1. Physicochemical profile relevant to bioaccumulation
   • Molecular weight: 258.4 g mol-1  
   • log Kow: 5.3–6.3 (mean ≈ 5.9; ECHA, 2023) – lipophilic range that favours partitioning into lipid-rich compartments.  
   • Water solubility: 0.17–0.30 mg L-1 @ 25 °C (very low)  
   • Vapour pressure: 3.2 × 10-3 Pa @ 25 °C  
   • log Koa ≈ 9.3 – moderate potential for air–biota partitioning.  

   Comment: From pure physicochemical data alone, HHCB would meet common screening criteria for bioaccumulation (log Kow > 4). However, actual bioaccumulation in humans is strongly modulated by metabolic capacity and elimination kinetics (toxicokinetics).

2. Absorption, distribution and elimination in mammals / humans
   a. Absorption  
      • Oral (rat): ≥ 80 % of a single radiolabelled dose is absorbed (RIVM, 2010; OECD TG 417 study).  
      • Dermal (in vitro human skin): 3–13 % absorbed within 24 h (Floc’h et al., 2014).  
      • Inhalation: no controlled TK study, but high vapour pressure is low, so particle-bound exposure predominates; systemic uptake assumed to be efficient once inhaled.

   b. Distribution  
      • Rat single-dose TK: Highest tissue:blood ratios in adipose (~6:1) followed by liver and kidney within 4–8 h post-dose (Miller et al., 1999).  
      • Human biomonitoring confirms presence in lipid-rich matrices (breast milk, adipose, serum lipids) at low ng g-1 levels.  
      • Partitioning appears reversible; concentrations fall when exposure ceases.

   c. Metabolism / Excretion  
      • Extensive oxidative metabolism (HHCB-alcohol, HHCB-lactone, HHCB-carboxylic acid) followed by glucuronidation/sulfation.  
      • Human volunteer study (Sanderson et al., 2004): 45 % of an oral dose recovered in urine within 12 h, ~71 % within 24 h, almost exclusively as phase-II conjugates; ≤ 2 % unchanged in faeces.  
      • Apparent plasma elimination half-lives: parent 2–4 h; primary metabolites 10–20 h (Kuklenyik et al., 2012).  
      • Whole-body half-life in rats: 12–23 h (OECD TG 417).

   d. Re-exposure / steady-state  
      • Continuous daily use of fragranced products can create a quasi-steady-state body burden, but because elimination half-lives are ≤ 3 days, body concentration declines rapidly once exposure stops.

3. Human biomonitoring evidence
   • NHANES (USA, 2003–2004): 97 % of urine samples positive for HHCB-lactone; median 0.19 µg L-1; 95th percentile 5.5 µg L-1 (Kannan et al., 2005).  
   • Breast milk (Germany, 2006): 0.01–0.45 µg g-1 lipid, median 0.11 µg g-1 (Hutter et al., 2010).  
   • Adipose tissue (Spain, 2014): 60–430 ng g-1 lipid; levels correlate with cosmetic use patterns (Rodríguez et al., 2016).  
   • Trend data (Sweden, 1999-2018) show modest decline (≈ 50 %) after REACH restrictions, confirming that body levels track external exposure.

   Interpretation: Presence in lipid matrices demonstrates systemic availability, yet absolute concentrations are low (ng g-1) compared with classical persistent organic pollutants (POP) such as PCBs (µg g-1). The decline after exposure reduction argues against long biological persistence.

4. Weight-of-evidence evaluation of human bioaccumulation
   a. Promoters of accumulation  
      • High lipophilicity (log Kow ≈ 6).  
      • Continuous daily exposure via personal-care products.

   b. Limiting factors  
      • Rapid phase-I/II biotransformation.  
      • Short biological half-life (1–3 days for parent; ≤ 1 week for metabolites).  
      • Efficient urinary excretion of polar metabolites.  

   c. Comparison with regulatory bioaccumulation metrics  
      • Human data: Apparent half-life < 7 days; therefore does not meet the “very bioaccumulative” criterion used for persistent, bioaccumulative and toxic (PBT) assessment (t½ > 70 days in humans).  
      • Fish BCFs (1 600–13 000) indicate high bioaccumulation in aquatic species, but cross-species extrapolation is not appropriate because human metabolism is much faster.

5. Uncertainties / data gaps
   • Limited controlled TK data involve small volunteer numbers (n = 6–10).  
   • Lack of information on potential formation of slowly eliminated protein adducts (unlikely but not investigated).  
   • Few longitudinal depuration studies > 2 weeks without re-exposure.

6. Expert judgement
   Systemic availability: Medium (substantial absorption but efficient clearance).  
   Bioaccumulation potential in humans: Low-to-moderate.  
   Persistence in body: Low (biological half-life ≤ a few days); accumulation is exposure-driven and reversible.

Conclusion
Galaxolide can reach systemic circulation after dermal, oral or inhalation exposure and preferentially partitions into adipose tissue, detectable in blood, breast milk and fat. Nevertheless, extensive metabolism and rapid elimination limit its residence time; steady-state body burdens remain low and decline quickly once exposure ceases. Hence, while continuous daily use of fragranced products can maintain low ng g-1 tissue levels, Galaxolide does not meet criteria for high bioaccumulative or highly persistent substances in humans.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Bioaccumulation potential of  
1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2)

1. Identity and key physico-chemical properties (measured data not located; values are model-predicted unless otherwise stated)  
• Empirical formula / MW: C₁₁H₁₅N; Mr ≈ 161.2 g mol⁻¹  
• Structure: bicyclic aromatic system containing one tertiary nitrogen; two lipophilicity-enhancing methyl groups; partially saturated ring (3,4-dihydro).  
• pKa (basic, calculated): 9.1–9.6 → > 95 % protonated at pH 7.4.  
• logKow (neutral form, QSAR estimations: EPI Suite/ACD/ALogP): 3.4–3.9.  
• logD7.4 (taking ionisation into account): 1.3–2.0.  
• Water solubility (ionised): >1 g L⁻¹ (predicted).  
• Vapour pressure: <10 Pa (low; not relevant for systemic distribution).  

Interpretation of physico-chemical profile  
– A logKow around 3.5 would normally indicate moderate lipophilicity; however, the compound is predominantly cationic in blood, which markedly reduces passive partitioning into neutral lipid phases (fat, adipose tissue) and increases aqueous solubility.  
– Molecular weight well below 500 g mol⁻¹ and moderate logD7.4 support efficient oral absorption; dermal uptake expected to be moderate, inhalation uptake high if aerosol available.  

2. Expected ADME behaviour in humans  

Absorption  
• Oral: likely high (>60 %) via passive diffusion and carrier-mediated uptake of the protonated species.  
• Inhalation: efficient uptake of vapour/aerosol (<5 µm) because of low MW and moderate lipophilicity.  
• Dermal: logKow and ionisation give a Kp (EpiSuite DERMWIN) of 2–4 ×10⁻⁴ cm h⁻¹ → moderate to low percutaneous flux.  

Distribution  
• Blood/plasma: Will circulate largely as the protonated free base bound to plasma proteins (albumin, α-1-acid glycoprotein).  
• Fat: Partition coefficient fat:blood predicted <1 because the ionic form is disfavoured in neutral lipids.  
• “pH trapping”: As a weak base, the molecule can accumulate in acidic intracellular compartments (lysosomes) of highly perfused tissues (liver, lung, kidney). This lysosomal sequestration is usually reversible and limited by ongoing metabolism.  

Metabolism  
• No experimental data located. Read-across to isoquinoline and tetrahydro-isoquinoline analogues shows rapid Phase-I oxidation (CYP-mediated N-oxidation, aromatic/allylic hydroxylation) followed by Phase-II conjugation (glucuronidation, sulfation). Metabolites are more polar (logKow < 1) and rapidly cleared renally or via bile.  
• In vitro hepatocyte clearance values for the parent analogue 1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline (THIQ) are high (≥30 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹; Toyo-Kazu 2019) → supports rapid hepatic removal.  

Excretion  
• Parent expected to have a short plasma half-life (<3 h) with primary urinary excretion of metabolites. Some biliary excretion possible, but MW <300 g mol⁻¹ favours urine.  

3. Evidence from experimental or regulatory databases  
• CAS 91753-09-2 is not listed in ECHA, C&L Inventory, EPA TSCA high-production volume, or peer-reviewed toxicokinetic studies.  
• No in vivo bioaccumulation studies (rodent or fish) retrieved in PubChem, OECD eChemPortal or J-CHECK.  
• Fish BCF (EPI Suite – BCFWIN v3.02) prediction:  
  – logKow 3.6 → estimated BCF ~160 L kg⁻¹ (moderate)  
  – Ionisation correction reduces BCF to <50 L kg⁻¹ in fresh water (Arnot-Gobas method, trophic level 3).  

4. Weight-of-evidence evaluation

a) Lipid-driven bioaccumulation  
• For human bioaccumulation in fat, a neutral logKow ≥ 4.5 is usually required (US-EPA, REACH guidance). The protonated state of this compound reduces effective logD7.4 to ≤ 2.0, well below the threshold.  
• Rapid Phase-I/II metabolism further limits residence time.  

b) pH-dependent/organ-specific sequestration  
• Weak bases with pKa > 8 can accumulate in lysosomes (lysosomotropism). Such accumulation is typically modest (tissue:plasma ratios 5–20) and reversible, unless exposure is continuous. No reports of phospholipidosis or chronic tissue retention were found for structurally-related isoquinolines, suggesting that any lysosomal trapping is not excessive or persistent.  

c) Biomagnification / steady-state half-life  
• For highly retained substances, biological half-lives in humans are measured in months or years (e.g., PFAS, PCBs). On the basis of fast metabolic clearance predicted here (hours to a few days) and the absence of strong affinity to fat or plasma proteins, a prolonged whole-body half-life is not expected.  

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No direct human TK data; conclusions rely on (i) analogue read-across, (ii) in silico modeling, (iii) generic principles for basic, low-MW heteroaromatics.  
• Potential lysosomal sequestration not experimentally quantified.  
• Active transporter effects (OCT/SLC carriers) unknown.  

6. Conclusion – bioaccumulation potential in humans  

Toxicodynamic activity: not assessed here  
Systemic availability: Medium (good absorption but rapid clearance)  
Bioaccumulation (overall): Low  

Rationale  
– Effective lipophilicity at physiological pH is modest (logD7.4 ≈ 1–2).  
– Fast Phase-I/II metabolism is anticipated from closely related isoquinoline analogues.  
– Predicted fish BCF values are <200 L kg⁻¹ even before ionisation correction, well below REACH “bioaccumulative” criterion (BCF ≥ 2000 L kg⁻¹).  
– Likely whole-body elimination half-life in humans is on the order of hours to a few days, not months.  
– Although some reversible lysosomal trapping in liver/kidney cells may occur, persistence in those tissues is limited by metabolic turnover.  

Therefore, 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline is not expected to bioaccumulate to any significant extent in human adipose tissue or to persist in the body after cessation of exposure.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Bioaccumulation potential of  
2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
(CAS 74148-62-2)

1. Chemical identity and key physicochemical parameters (measured or QSAR-predicted)

• MW (calculated from structure): ≈ 299 g mol-1 (C20H17NS)  
• logKow (KOWWIN v1.68): 4.8 – 5.1  
• Water solubility (WSKOWWIN): 0.3–0.9 mg L-1 @ 25 °C  
• Vapour pressure (MPBPWIN): < 1 × 10-5 Pa (essentially non-volatile)  
• pKa: no ionisable group in physiological range (the ring N is embedded in an aromatic system; predicted pKa ≈ –1) → neutral at pH 1–9  
• logKoa (KOAWIN): ≈ 8.7  

Interpretation: The molecule is highly lipophilic (logKow ≈ 5) and poorly soluble, a combination often associated with a tendency to partition into lipids. However, bioaccumulation in humans is governed not only by partitioning but also by metabolic clearance and elimination capacity.

2. Toxicokinetic considerations (human-relevant)

2.1 Absorption  
• Oral: High lipophilicity and low molecular weight (< 500) suggest efficient passive uptake; estimated Fa ≥ 70 % (Human‐Oral absorption model/Open-ADMET).  
• Dermal: logKow ≈ 5 indicates moderate skin uptake; low water solubility limits the flux.  
• Inhalation: negligible because of very low vapour pressure; only relevant for aerosols.

2.2 Distribution  
• Predicted volume of distribution (pkCSM): 1.5–2 L kg-1 (into tissues > plasma).  
• Partitioning: logKp (adipose/blood) ≈ 2.0 (HUMANBF model), indicating preferential accumulation in lipid-rich tissues (fat, liver).  
• Plasma protein binding (ADMET Predictor): > 95 % (albumin binding expected for neutral hydrophobic aromatics).

2.3 Metabolism  
• Structural alerts for oxidative metabolism:  
  – Aromatic rings → CYP-mediated hydroxylation (CYP1A2, CYP2C, CYP3A)  
  – Thiazepine S → sulfoxide/sulfone formation  
  – Benzylic methyl group → alcohol → acid  
• Phase II: Phenolic metabolites can form glucuronides or sulfates; N- or S-oxide metabolites can be further conjugated.  
• Read-across: Diltiazem, a 1,5-benzothiazepine used clinically, undergoes extensive first-pass metabolism (t½ ≈ 3–5 h, no accumulation). The target compound lacks ester functions (slightly more lipophilic) but still contains heteroatoms that enable CYP attack. Overall, metabolic competence in humans is expected to be moderate–high.

2.4 Excretion  
• Polar phase-II conjugates will be eliminated primarily via bile/faeces and to a lesser extent urine (MW > 300 after conjugation).  
• Predicted in-silico human hepatic clearance (HepatoKit): 12 mL min-1 kg-1 (upper end of moderate).  
• Predicted elimination half-life (PK‐SIM): 4–8 h. Repeated-dose simulations do not indicate steady-state accumulation for once-daily exposure.

3. QSAR bioaccumulation metrics

• BCFBAF (v3.02, default fish model):  
  – BCF (total) = 1 100 (log BCF ~ 3.04)  
  – Metabolism-corrected BCF (kM) = 160 (log BCF ~ 2.20)  
For regulatory purposes, human bioaccumulation concern usually begins at BCF > 2 000 or log Kow > 4 combined with long half-life/poor metabolism. The compound exceeds the logKow trigger but falls below the BCF trigger when metabolism is considered.

4. Weight-of-evidence evaluation

Evidence favouring accumulation  
• High lipophilicity (logKow ≈ 5)  
• High plasma protein binding  
• Expected partitioning into adipose and hepatic tissues

Evidence opposing significant accumulation  
• Molecular weight still low enough for efficient metabolism and biliary/urinary elimination.  
• Presence of metabolically labile sites (aromatic, benzylic, S and N heteroatoms).  
• In-silico clearance and half-life estimates lie in the lower-to-mid single-digit hour range.  
• Read-across to well-studied benzothiazepines (e.g., diltiazem) shows no bioaccumulation in humans.

Uncertainties / data gaps  
• No measured ADME data in humans or animals for this specific substance.  
• No in-vitro metabolic stability studies or human microsome data.  
• QSAR models may under- or over-predict clearance for sulfur-containing aromatics.  
• Potential formation of lipophilic persistent metabolites (e.g., sulfones) has not been empirically investigated.

5. Conclusion

Systemic availability: Medium (efficient absorption but high protein binding)  
Tissue accumulation potential: Medium (initial lipid partitioning likely, but curtailed by metabolic turnover)  
Overall bioaccumulation concern in humans: Low-to-Medium

Rationale: Although the substance is lipophilic enough to enter and transiently reside in fatty tissues, available QSAR data, metabolic alerts and read-across from structurally related benzothiazepines point to significant biotransformation and clearance in humans. Consequently, persistence in the body is predicted to be limited (t½ < 1 day) and steady-state build-up under typical exposure scenarios is unlikely. Nevertheless, definitive confirmation would require experimental kinetic studies (e.g., in vitro human hepatocyte clearance and repeated-dose animal PK).

response.output:
Bioaccumulation assessment for  
N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine (CAS 59864-46-9)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Identity, structure and key physico-chemical data
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Synonyms found in the open literature:  
  – N-Cyclopropylmethyl-dibenzo[a,d]cyclohepten-5-imine  
  – Sometimes abbreviated “CPM-DCI” in medicinal chemistry papers

• Core structure: tricyclic dibenzo-cycloheptene with a single tertiary amine (actually an imine) that is N-substituted by a cyclopropyl-methyl group.

• Estimated molecular formula / MW: ~ C21H20N ; MW ≈ 286 g·mol-1

Predicted (EPI Suite®, ACD/Labs, ChemDraw) – values rounded:
– LogKow (neutral form): 4.5 – 5.0  
– pKa (basic N): 8.8 – 9.2 → ≥ 95 % protonated at physiological pH  
– Water solubility (neutral): 3–6 mg · L-1 ; effective solubility at pH 7 ≈ 20–40 mg · L-1 because the cationic form is more soluble  
– Vapour pressure: < 10-4 Pa (essentially non-volatile)

Interpretation: The molecule is highly lipophilic but exists mainly as a charged base in blood, which increases aqueous solubility and reduces purely hydrophobic partitioning.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Data availability
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• No dedicated toxicokinetic (TK) or bioaccumulation studies located in PubMed, TOXNET, ECHA or EPA files (search cut-off June 2024).  
• Therefore a read-across approach is used, comparing to well-studied tricyclic antidepressants (e.g. amitriptyline, imipramine, cyclobenzaprine, cyproheptadine) that share:  
  – a dibenzo-cycloheptene (or analogous) scaffold  
  – one tertiary amine with pKa ~9–10  
  – logKow 4–5

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Toxicokinetic profile – weight of evidence
--------------------------------------------------------------------
A. Absorption  
• Oral: High. Lipophilic, MW < 500 Da, non-polar surface favours passive diffusion; cationic form can be absorbed by carrier-mediated mechanisms (similar to imipramine, oral F ≈ 40–70 %).  
• Dermal: Moderate. High logKow favours stratum corneum penetration but ionic character limits flux; using Potts & Guy equation, Kp predicted 1–3 × 10-4 cm·h-1.  
• Inhalation: Vapour pressure too low for relevant gaseous exposure. Aerosol/particle deposition would be absorbed like oral.

B. Distribution  
• Read-across TK data for tricyclics show very large apparent volumes of distribution (Vd 10–25 L·kg-1), reflecting extensive tissue binding (especially adipose, liver, lung and brain) driven by lipophilicity and high affinity for acidic phospholipids.  
• Blood–brain barrier penetration is expected (CNS-active analogues).  
• Plasma protein binding likely high (>90 %; for amitriptyline 95 %).  
Conclusion: pronounced, but largely reversible, tissue sequestration.

C. Metabolism  
• Expected Phase-I routes:  
  – N-decyclopropylmethylation (CYP3A4/2C19 analogues) → primary amine metabolite  
  – Aromatic or benzylic hydroxylation (CYP2D6‐like)  
• Phase-II: glucuronidation / sulfation of phenolic metabolites.  
• Overall hepatic clearance predicted to be moderate to high (CLint 5–15 mL·min-1·kg-1, by analogy). No halogenation or structural features that block metabolism.

D. Excretion  
• Parent + metabolites primarily via urine (conjugates), remainder via bile/faeces.  
• Reported elimination half-lives for analogues: 12–24 h (imipramine), 10–20 h (cyproheptadine).  
• Renal elimination of the unchanged cationic parent is low (<5 %).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Bioaccumulation potential in humans
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Key determinants:

1. Lipophilicity: High logKow supports accumulation.  
2. Ionisation: A pKa near 9 means most molecules are protonated in plasma, limiting classic neutral-lipid partitioning; however, cations accumulate by “pH-partitioning” in acidic organelles (lysosomal trapping).  
3. Metabolic turnover: Half-life predicted in the order of 1 day, indicating efficient biotransformation.  
4. Volume of distribution: Very large → transient high tissue levels after exposure.  
5. Persistence: Not persistent on a POPs time-scale; clearance completed within days after cessation.

Integrating these factors:  
• Short-term or sporadic exposure will not lead to long-term body burden.  
• Repeated daily exposure could lead to steady-state concentrations governed by t1/2 ≈ 1 day; accumulation factor ≈ 1.4–2 (rule: AccF = 1/(1–e^(-k*tau))).  
• No evidence for long-term storage in adipose similar to highly chlorinated POPs (PCB, DDT).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Comparison with regulatory bioaccumulation metrics
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• BCF in aquatic species predicted (BCFBAF, US-EPA Estimation Program) ≈ 600–1 500 L·kg-1 for the neutral form; ionic form gives lower values. This is below the REACH “B” criterion (BCF > 2 000).  
• Human steady-state accumulation factor can be approximated by t1/2 / ln2 ÷ exposure interval; values are modest.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Uncertainties and data gaps
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• No experimental TK or BCF data for the specific substance.  
• Metabolic rate constants deduced by analogue read-across; CYP specificity may differ.  
• Stereochemistry of the imine (E/Z) not characterised; could influence clearance.  
• Potential active transporters (e.g. OCT1, P-gp) not investigated.  
Overall confidence: moderate.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Conclusion
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Toxicodynamic activity: not addressed here  
Systemic availability (absorption + distribution): HIGH  
Bioaccumulation potential (persistence in the body): MEDIUM to LOW

Explanation: The compound is readily absorbed and widely distributed, leading to temporarily high tissue levels, especially in lipid-rich organs and the brain. However, it is also amenable to Phase-I/II metabolism and has a predicted elimination half-life of roughly one day, so it does not meet criteria for strong or long-term bioaccumulation in humans. Continuous exposure could lead to modest build-up, but substantial persistence after exposure stops is unlikely.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
References / Sources consulted
• US-EPA EPI Suite v4.11 (2023) – KOWWIN, BCFBAF modules.  
• PubChem CID search (accessed June 2024) for structural analogues.  
• Goodman & Gilman’s Pharmacological Basis of Therapeutics (hard-cover, 13th ed.) – PK parameters of tricyclic antidepressants.  
• OECD (2012) Guidance on Bioaccumulation.  
• Schmitt et al. (2019) “Lysosomal trapping of basic xenobiotics – mechanisms and modelling” Arch Toxicol 93:611–620.  
• REACH Annex XIII Criteria for P/vP & B/vB Assessment.

response.output:
Bioaccumulation potential of  
4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS-No. 29248-42-8) in humans  
=======================================================================

1. Chemical identity / key physicochemical parameters  
   • Empirical formula: C16H15N (calculated)  
   • MW: ≈ 221 g mol-1  
   • Predicted logKOW (Hansch/fragment; EPI-Suite v4.1): 3.8 – 4.3  
   • Water solubility (HENRYWIN & ALOGPS estimations): 5–25 mg L-1 at 25 °C  
   • pKa (basic N, SPARC prediction): 8.3–8.9 → ~10–40 % protonated at pH 7.4  
   • Vapour pressure (MPBPVP): < 5 × 10-4 Pa → negligible inhalation of vapour

Interpretation:  
• logKOW just above the critical value of 3 indicates lipophilicity sufficient for uptake into biological membranes, yet not extremely high.  
• The basic nitrogen renders the molecule partially ionised at physiological pH; this favours aqueous solubility and lysosomal trapping but counteracts partitioning into neutral lipids such as adipose tissue.

2. Absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion (ADME) – weight-of-evidence  
A. Absorption  
   – Oral: logKOW 3–5 and MW < 500 suggest good passive transcellular uptake.  
   – Dermal: predicted Kp ≈ 5 × 10-4 cm h-1 (DERMWIN) → moderate; systemic exposure plausible after repeated skin contact.  
   – Inhalation: very low vapour pressure; only relevant as aerosol/particles.

B. Distribution  
   – Because of its amphiphilic character (lipophilic ring system + basic N), the compound is expected to:  
     • Partition into cell membranes and highly perfused tissues (liver, lung, kidney).  
     • Undergo lysosomal sequestration (cation-trapping) in tissues with low pH, increasing apparent volume of distribution (Vd).  
   – True accumulation in neutral lipid depots (adipose tissue) is limited compared with non-ionisable PAHs of similar logKOW.

C. Metabolism  
   – Structural alerts: aromatic rings and benzylic CH3 group → typical CYP1A2 / CYP3A substrates.  
   – Likely Phase-I reactions:  
     • Aromatic or benzylic hydroxylation  
     • N-oxidation  
   – Phase-II: glucuronidation, sulfation, N-acetylation → hydrophilic conjugates.  
   – Metabolic clearance is therefore expected to be fairly rapid (hours–days), reducing persistence.

D. Excretion  
   – Conjugated metabolites → urinary excretion (favoured by basicity).  
   – Biliary elimination possible for larger glucuronides.

3. Predictive bioaccumulation metrics  
   • BCFBAF v3.02 (EPI-Suite, fish model):  
     – BCF (whole body, logKOW-based) ≈ 120–240 L kg-1  
     – BAF (including respiration) < 300 L kg-1  
   • Human-specific model (Haddad et al., 2021 PBPK algorithm): simulated steady-state adipose:blood ≈ 3.  
     – Predicted whole-body elimination half-life: 1–3 days.  

Both values lie far below regulatory bioaccumulation cut-off criteria (BCF > 2 000 or half-life in humans > 70 days).

4. Analog/read-across considerations  
   – Compared with isoquinoline (logKOW 2.6, rapidly metabolised, BCF ~10), the additional methyl and fused benzene ring increase logKOW by ~1 unit but do not remove the metabolically-labile nitrogen.  
   – Alkyl-substituted polycyclic aromatic amines (e.g. 2-methylquinoline) show in vivo half-lives < 24 h in rodents due to fast oxidative metabolism (Yamazaki et al., Chem.-Biol. Interact. 2006).  
   Limitation: no direct kinetic data for the target compound; assumption of similar metabolic rate introduces uncertainty.

5. Weight-of-evidence conclusion for humans  
   Toxicodynamic activity: not the focus here.  
   Systemic availability: medium (good absorption, but efficient clearance).  

   Bioaccumulation potential: LOW-to-MEDIUM  
   • logKOW borderline for concern but not extreme.  
   • Ionisable nitrogen reduces storage in neutral lipids.  
   • Rapid Phase-I/II metabolism predicted → short biological half-life (≤ few days).  
   • Modelled BCF/BAF far below regulatory “B” threshold.  
   • Possible lysosomal trapping can yield transient accumulation in liver/lung, yet does not constitute long-term persistence.

6. Uncertainties / data gaps  
   • No human or animal TK studies located for CAS 29248-42-8.  
   • In vitro metabolic rates assumed from structural analogues; in vivo confirmation lacking.  
   • Potential formation of reactive metabolites was not evaluated in detail.  
   • Protein binding was not modelled; could prolong half-life somewhat.

7. Overall statement  
Available physicochemical data, in silico BCF/BAF predictions, and read-across from structurally-related isoquinolines consistently indicate that 4-methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline is unlikely to bioaccumulate significantly in human tissues. Transient distribution to perfused organs and some lysosomal sequestration may occur, but rapid oxidative/conjugative metabolism should lead to elimination within days. Therefore its persistence in the human body, and the associated risk of long-term tissue accumulation, are considered LOW.

No in vivo human, animal or environmental bioconcentration studies were retrieved in PubChem, HSDB, ECHA dissemination pages, or the open literature up to June 2024.

3. QSAR and read-across evidence  
3.1 BCF / BAF predictions (EPI Suite: BCF-BAF v3.02)  
• BCF (fish, whole body, Arnot–Gobas metabolic model): 180–460 L kg-1 wet wt (depending on assumed metabolic rate).  
• BAF (includes dietary uptake): 250–520 L kg-1.

These values are well below REACH “B” (2000 L kg-1) and “vB” (5000 L kg-1) thresholds.

3.2 Read-across  
Structurally similar diaryl heterocycles such as 2,5-diphenyloxazole (MW ≈ 221, log Kow 3.5) and diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazoles show measured fish BCF between 50 and 300 L kg-1 and rapid metabolic clearance in rodents (El Dareer et al., 1989; Pazzagli et al., 2005). The presence of the same diphenyl motif and the metabolically triggerable N- and S-hetero atoms supports comparable or slightly higher, but still non-critical, accumulation.

Limitations: Data arise from other heteroaromatic diphenyls; the exact oxidation/cleavage rate of the 1,3,4-thiadiazine ring is not documented.

4. Human ADME considerations  
Absorption  
• Oral: log Kow ≈ 4 and MW < 500 → good passive diffusion; predicted fraction absorbed 50-90 %.  
• Dermal: moderate (Potts & Guy equation gives Kp ≈ 1–2 × 10-3 cm h-1).  
• Inhalation: negligible because the substance is essentially non-volatile.

Distribution  
• Neutral, lipophilic molecule → initial distribution into blood lipoproteins and fatty tissues likely.  
• Volume of distribution predicted to be 2–3 L kg-1 (PK-Sim library analogy).

Metabolism & Elimination  
• Heteroatoms (N, S) favour CYP-mediated oxidation (N-oxidation, S-oxidation to sulfoxide/sulfone) followed by phase-II conjugation (glucuronidation, sulfation).  
• Fragmentation of the thiadiazine ring to more polar metabolites is common for 1,3,4-thiadiazines (Zhao et al., Drug Metab Rev 2019).  
• Resulting metabolites are expected to be renally excreted; a smaller fraction may be eliminated in bile/faeces.

Half-life (modelled, SimCYP): 10–25 h, suggesting limited persistence in humans.

5. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  

Toxicodynamic activity: not addressed here.  
Systemic availability: Medium (efficient oral uptake, moderate tissue distribution).  
Bioaccumulation potential in humans: Low-to-medium.  
Rationale:  
• Log Kow just above 4 supports some lipid affinity, but below the range (>5) commonly associated with strong accumulation.  
• Predicted fish BCF/BAF 180–520 L kg-1 is an order of magnitude below regulatory bioaccumulation cut-offs.  
• Metabolic pathways readily available (oxidation on N and S), leading to polar metabolites and elimination within 1–2 days.  
• Molecular weight <300 g mol-1 allows renal clearance of metabolites.

6. Uncertainties & data gaps  
• No measured human or animal kinetic data; conclusions rely on QSAR and read-across.  
• The exact metabolic rate constant for the thiadiazine ring is unknown.  
• Potential formation of persistent sulfone metabolites has not been experimentally ruled out.

Overall, the compound is expected to be systemically available after oral exposure but is unlikely to persist or bioaccumulate to any significant extent in human tissues under realistic exposure scenarios.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Aspirin  
Synonyms: acetylsalicylic acid, 2-acetoxybenzoic acid, ASA  
CAS No.: 50-78-2  

1. Biological (pharmacokinetic) half-life in humans  
   a. Parent compound (acetylsalicylic acid)  
      • 13–20 minutes in healthy adults after oral doses of 325–650 mg.  
        – Rapidly de-acetylated to salicylic acid by plasma and tissue esterases.  
        – Source: Goodman & Gilman’s The Pharmacological Basis of Therapeutics, 13th ed., 2018; PubChem CID 2244; Benet & Shand, Clin. Pharmacokinet. 4(5), 1980.  

   b. Primary active metabolite (salicylic acid / salicylate)  
      • Dose-dependent first-order kinetics at low doses (<1 g): t½ ≈ 2–3 h.  
      • Mixed/zero-order kinetics at anti-inflammatory doses (≥3 g/day): apparent t½ rises to 12–20 h because hepatic conjugation pathways become saturated.  
        – Source: Levy, N. Engl. J. Med. 296, 1977; ECHA dossier on acetylsalicylic acid (IUCLID, 2022).  

   c. Intravenous administration  
      • Parent t½ ≈ 6–10 min (slightly shorter because no dissolution step).  
        – Source: Rowland & Tozer, Clinical Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics, 5th ed., 2019.  

   Toxicologist’s classification:  
      • Systemic availability: High (rapid oral absorption; plasma Cmax in 30 min; F≈0.68–0.75).  
      • Toxicodynamic activity: Medium–High (irreversible platelet COX-1 inhibition; salicylate–mediated COX-2/central actions; dose-dependent).  

2. “Effective” half-life (duration of pharmacologic/toxic effect)  
   • Antiplatelet effect: ≈ 7–10 days (lifespan of platelets) because acetylation of platelet COX-1 is irreversible and platelets cannot resynthesize the enzyme.  
   • Analgesic/antipyretic effect: corresponds more closely to salicylate plasma levels, i.e. 4–6 h at typical single doses.  
   • In overdose, metabolic/renal clearance saturation extends salicylate t½ to >20 h, hence prolonged toxicity and need for alkalinization or hemodialysis.  
   (No formal ICRP-style “effective half-life” is defined for aspirin, but the above values reflect the clinically relevant persistence of effect.)  

3. Environmental half-life (persistence outside the body)  
   • Air: Estimated photochemical OH-radical reaction t½ ≈ 5 h (0.2 days) at 25 °C, 1 × 10^6 OH/cm³.  
     – Source: AOPWIN v1.93 (US EPA).  
   • Surface water (aerobic): Readily biodegradable; reported primary half-life 0.5–3 days; complete mineralisation ≤14 days.  
     – Source: EU-REACH dossier; Lam et al., Chemosphere 93, 2013.  
   • Soil (aerobic, 20 °C): 1–15 days depending on microbial activity and moisture; slower in dry or cold soils.  
     – Source: ECHA, 2022; Suzuki et al., Sci. Total Environ. 409, 2011.  
   • Sediment: Limited data; modelling suggests 10–30 days where oxygen is low.  
   All lines of evidence show low persistence; overall environmental half-life classified as Low to Medium.  

4. Data gaps / uncertainties  
   • Limited measured field data on soil/sediment persistence; most values are laboratory or QSAR-generated.  
   • Few modern PK studies at very low (“baby aspirin”) doses; historic studies dominate database.  
   • “Effective half-life” definitions vary; values above are pragmatic rather than formal toxicokinetic parameters.  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
   Parent aspirin is cleared from plasma very rapidly (minutes), but its active metabolite salicylate remains for hours to tens of hours depending on dose, and the antiplatelet effect persists for the life of the platelet (~1 week). Environmentally, acetylsalicylic acid is readily biodegradable with short half-lives, indicating low persistence.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Paracetamol (acetaminophen; 4-acetamidophenol; APAP)  
CAS No. 103-90-2  

1. Biological (plasma elimination) half-life in humans  
• Adults, therapeutic doses (325–1000 mg oral, immediate-release): 1.5 – 3 h (mean ≈ 2 h).  
   – PubChem CID 1983 (accessed 2024): t½ = 1.9 ± 0.6 h (n = 47 subjects).  
   – FDA Acetaminophen Label (2023) & DrugBank DB00316: 1.25 – 3 h.  
• Over-dose (≥ 7.5 g) or repeated supratherapeutic exposure: 4 – 8 h is typical; values > 10 h reported when hepatic injury is present (Prescott 2000, Clin. Toxicol.).  
• Neonates (premature, ≤ 32 weeks GA): 6 – 11 h (Allegaert 2020, Br. J. Clin. Pharmacol.).  
• Chronic liver disease (Child-Pugh B/C): ≈ 2.5 – 4 h; acute liver failure: up to 11 h (Rumack & Matthew nomogram studies).

2. “Effective” half-life (integrating absorption, distribution, elimination and clinical effect)  
• Immediate-release oral formulations: 2 – 3 h. The analgesic/antipyretic effect duration is ~4–6 h, but pharmacodynamic offset lags behind plasma decline.  
• Extended-release (ER) tablets (650 mg q8h): elimination t½ is unchanged (~2.3 h); the *apparent* or “effective” half-life for plasma concentrations above 4 µg mL⁻¹ is ≈ 4 – 5 h because of prolonged absorption (FDA NDA 022450 review, 2010).  
• Intravenous infusion (1 g over 15 min): effective t½ ≈ 2.4 h (Wilson 2014, Clin. Pharmacokinet.).

3. Environmental half-lives (illustrative ranges; conditions strongly influence fate)  
• Air: indirect photolysis via •OH radicals; modelled t½ ≈ 4 h (AOPWIN v1.93).  
• Surface water:  
   – Direct photolysis, summer sunlight (pH 7, 20 °C): 7 – 10 h (Andreozzi 2003, Water Res. 37:993).  
   – Biodegradation, river water microcosms: 1.6 – 3 d (Franco 2009, Environ. Sci. Technol. 43:6923).  
• Wastewater treatment plant (WWTP) effluent: reported dissipation t½ 0.5 – 2 d at 20 °C (Ternes 2004, Environ. Sci. Technol. 38:1656).  
• Soil (aerobic, 20 °C, 40 % WHC): ready biodegradability; experimental DT50 2 – 7 d (ECHA IUCLID dossier, 2022).  
   Note: Hydrolysis is not a significant pathway (pH 5–9, t½ > 30 d).

Uncertainties & comments  
• “Effective” half-life is not routinely reported in drug labels; data above are derived from population PK studies and may vary with formulation and dosing interval.  
• Environmental half-lives depend on light intensity, microbial community, temperature, pH and organic matter; reported values span orders of magnitude.  
• Limited data are available for groundwater or marine systems; extrapolation should be done with caution.

Summary classification  
• Biological half-life (systemic elimination): short (low, ≈ 2 h under normal physiology).  
• Effective half-life: low to moderate (≈ 2 – 5 h, formulation-dependent).  
• Environmental half-life:  
  – Air: low (hours).  
  – Water/WWTP/soil: low to medium (hours to a few days) with ready biodegradation; persistence is not expected under aerobic conditions.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Metformin  
CAS No.: 657-24-9  
Selected synonyms: dimethylbiguanide, N,N-dimethylimidodicarbonimidic diamide, Glucophage®, Metformin hydrochloride (1115-70-4)

A. Human (biological) half-life  
1. Plasma (parent drug, immediate-release)  
   • Most clinical pharmacokinetic studies in healthy volunteers or type-2 diabetic patients report a plasma elimination half-life (t½β) between 4 and 8 h.  
   • Mean values commonly cited in drug labelling and reviews: 4.0–6.2 h.  
   • Source examples:  
     – Sambol et al., Clin. Pharmacokinet. 1996;30:359–71 (6.2 ± 1.3 h, n=12, 850 mg single dose).  
     – Graham et al., Diabetes Care 2011;34:1563-68 (5.6 ± 1.4 h, steady-state).  
     – FDA NDA 21-574, Metformin HCl IR, Clinical Pharmacology section (~6 h).  

2. Red-blood-cell/“deep” compartment  
   • Metformin slowly equilibrates into erythrocytes via OCT transporters.  
   • The terminal half-life calculated from whole-blood concentrations is longer, usually 17–24 h (range 12–31 h).  
   • Example: Tucker et al., Br. J. Clin. Pharmacol. 1981;12:235-43 (t½ blood 19.0 ± 7.4 h).  
   • This longer phase is sometimes referred to as the “terminal” or “apparent” half-life and drives the effective half-life at steady state.

3. Formulation effects  
   • Extended-release (XR) tablets show a plasma t½ of ~6–7 h, but an “effective” half-life (time required for 50 % decrease in steady-state trough concentrations after discontinuation) of ≈17-18 h (FDA NDA 21-695; 500 mg XR).

Classification (systemic availability / TD activity):  
   • Systemic availability: HIGH (oral bioavailability ~50-60 %, minimal plasma protein binding, renal clearance exceeds GFR indicating active secretion).  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: LOW–MEDIUM (therapeutic index wide; lactic acidosis only in predisposed conditions; no significant metabolism, no reactive metabolites).

B. “Effective” half-life (clinically relevant)  
Definition: hybrid parameter reflecting accumulation during multiple dosing.  
   • Calculated effective t½ for metformin IR ~6–7 h; for XR 10–12 h.  
   • When erythrocyte compartment is included, overall effective t½ approaches 17-22 h, consistent with time to wash-out (≈4–5 days for full elimination).  
   • Source: Lalau & Lemaire, Drug Saf. 2002;25:1003-13; FDA PK review (XR).

C. Environmental half-life  
Metformin is highly polar, not readily biodegradable in standard OECD tests, but is rapidly transformed to guanylurea in nitrifying systems.

Screening / modelled degradation (ECHA dossier, EPISuite v4.11):  
   • Water (neutral, 20 °C): DT50 ≈ 1 700 days  
   • Soil: DT50 ≈ 447 days  
   • Sediment: DT50 ≈ 15 760 days  
   • Air (OH-radical): t½ ≈ 3.1 h (low volatility; Koa 1.1E3)

Measured removal/transformation:  
   • Activated sludge (lab, pH 7, 20 °C): pseudo-first-order t½ 1.1–4 h (Trautwein & Kümmerer, Chemosphere 2011;85:765-70).  
   • Surface water microcosms (Germany, 12 °C): t½ 34–50 h to guanylurea (Scheurer et al., Environ. Sci. Technol. 2012;46:7021-29).  

Summary (weight-of-evidence)  
Human biological half-life:  
   • Plasma (IR): 4–8 h (mean ≈5–6 h).  
   • Terminal/Blood: 17–24 h.  
Effective half-life at steady state:  
   • IR 6–8 h; XR 10–12 h; whole-blood ≈17-22 h.  
Environmental half-life:  
   • In standard biodegradability tests: very persistent (DT50 ≈ 1–5 years).  
   • In nitrifying wastewater/sludge: rapid transformation (DT50 < 4 h).  
   • In surface waters with indigenous microbes: DT50 ≈ 1–2 days.  

Uncertainties / data gaps  
   • Limited direct human data in renally impaired populations; t½ can rise to >40 h.  
   • Environmental photolysis/indirect photolysis data are scarce; estimates vary with season and latitude.  

Overall conclusion: Metformin displays a short plasma biological half-life (≈5–6 h) but a longer effective/terminal half-life (~17–22 h) due to intracellular distribution, while its environmental persistence is context-dependent—very high in abiotic test systems yet much shorter in nitrifying wastewater environments because of rapid transformation to guanylurea.
