vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Half-life data for Atorvastatin (CAS No. 134523-00-5; synonyms: atorvastatin calcium, Lipitor, Sortis, CI-981) are summarized below.  Where possible, the original source or a secondary authoritative citation is given.  Values are expressed as arithmetic means (or medians) followed by the range reported in the cited study.  All half-lives refer to humans unless otherwise stated.

1. Biological (plasma/serum) elimination half-life  
   • Parent compound (atorvastatin acid)  
     – 14 h (range 11–30 h)  – US FDA Prescribing Information, 2023; European SmPC, 2022  
     – 13.2 ± 3.5 h (healthy male volunteers, 10 mg single dose, n = 12) – Lennernäs & McClure 2003, Clin. Pharmacokinet. 42:1141-1160  
     – 14.4 h (hyperlipidaemic patients, steady-state, 80 mg/day) – Black et al. 1998, Br. J. Clin. Pharmacol. 45:691-700  

   • Active metabolites (ortho- and para-hydroxy-atorvastatin & their lactones)  
     – 20–30 h pooled terminal t½ – US FDA Label; EMEA Assessment Report  
     – 19.8 ± 4.3 h (ortho-hydroxy) and 26.4 ± 6.1 h (para-hydroxy) – Lau et al. 2006, J. Clin. Pharmacol. 46:991-1000  

2. “Effective” half-life (i.e. pharmacodynamic half-life of HMG-CoA-reducing activity, reflecting parent + active metabolites)  
   • 20–30 h – cited by both US FDA and SmPC; based on in vivo cholesterol-synthesis inhibition assays  
   • Modelling of LDL-cholesterol fall after chronic dosing gives an average effective t½ of 25–30 h – G. K. Hovingh et al., 2015, Atherosclerosis 241:404-410  

   Interpretation: Even though systemic plasma levels of the parent drop by ~50 % in ∼14 h, the pharmacological effect persists for roughly a full day because active metabolites have longer half-lives and comparable inhibitory potency.

3. Environmental half-lives (outside the human body)  
   Empirical environmental fate data for atorvastatin are limited; most values come from laboratory simulation studies and model estimates compiled in the ECHA registration dossier and peer-reviewed environmental fate papers.

   Water (surface water, 20–25 °C, near-neutral pH)  
   • Photolysis, simulated sunlight: t½ ≈ 0.7–2 d (mean 1.3 d) – Latch et al. 2003, Environ. Sci. Technol. 37:3342-3350  
   • Dark conditions (hydrolysis + biodegradation): modelled t½ 15–20 d – EPI Suite 4.1 (BIOWIN/KOWWIN); confirmed by Vieno & Sillanpää 2014 review  

   STP (activated sludge)  
   • Primary biodegradation t½ 1–3 d; mineralisation incomplete (not readily biodegradable) – ECHA dossier study No. 201-004-5835  

   Soil (aerobic, 20 °C)  
   • Laboratory soil microcosms: DT50 28–45 d; DT90 90–140 d – Fono & Sedlak 2005, J. Agric. Food Chem. 53:5175-5182  

   Sediment (anaerobic)  
   • Modelled t½ >100 d – US EPA ECOSAR v2.0 estimate  

   Caveats: Environmental half-life values vary with light exposure, microbial activity, and matrix characteristics; values above should be regarded as order-of-magnitude estimates rather than fixed constants.

4. Summary and weight-of-evidence rating  
   • Biological half-life of parent atorvastatin in humans: ~14 h (medium uncertainty).  
   • Effective/pharmacodynamic half-life: 20–30 h owing to equipotent, longer-lived hydroxymetabolites (high confidence).  
   • Environmental half-life:  
     – Surface water: rapid photolysis (hours-days) but slower removal in dark (~2–3 weeks).  
     – Soil: weeks to a few months.  
     – Sediment: months (modelled).  Confidence medium-low due to sparse empirical data.

No evidence of radioactive isotopes is relevant; hence “effective half-life” is used in the pharmacodynamic sense rather than the radiological definition.

Key references  
1. US Food and Drug Administration (FDA). Atorvastatin Calcium Tablets, Prescribing Information (revised 2023).  
2. European Medicines Agency. Atorvastatin SmPC (Sortis), last updated 2022.  
3. Lennernäs, H. & McClure, R. (2003). Clinical Pharmacokinetics 42:1141-1160.  
4. Lau, Y. Y. et al. (2006). Journal of Clinical Pharmacology 46:991-1000.  
5. Latch, D. E. et al. (2003). Environmental Science & Technology 37:3342-3350.  
6. Fono, L. J., & Sedlak, D. L. (2005). Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry 53:5175-5182.  
7. ECHA Registration Dossier for Atorvastatin (accessed May 2024).  
8. EPI Suite™ v4.1 (US EPA, 2023).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Half-life information for Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2; synonyms: Deltacortisone, Meticorten) in humans and the environment

1. Biological (plasma elimination) half-life in humans  
   • Prednisone itself: 0.3 – 1.5 h  
     – Rapidly reduced by hepatic 11-β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase-1 to the active metabolite prednisolone.  
     – Reported mean t½ ≈ 0.6 h (range 0.3-1.5 h) in healthy adults after a single oral dose of 20 mg (Derendorf et al., 1993; FDA product label, 2020).  
   • Prednisolone (active metabolite): 2 – 4 h  
     – Mean t½ ≈ 3.2 h in adults with normal liver function (Goodman & Gilman, 13th ed.; ECHA Substance Dossier, 2023).  
     – In severe hepatic impairment the terminal t½ can increase to ~8 h (Bever et al., 1984).

2. Effective (pharmacodynamic/“biologic”) half-life  
   – This describes the duration of downstream glucocorticoid effects (gene transcription, protein turnover) rather than drug disappearance from plasma.  
   – Prednisone/prednisolone are classified as “intermediate-acting” corticosteroids with an effective t½ of 12 – 36 h (Katzung’s Basic & Clinical Pharmacology, 15th ed.; WHO Essential Medicines List notes).  
   – Clinical dosing regimens (once daily in the morning) are based on this longer effective half-life.

3. Environmental half-life (degradation / dissipation)  
   Data are scarcer and often come from laboratory simulation studies:  
   • Surface water photolysis: t½ ≈ 14-20 h at pH 7, 25 °C under simulated sunlight (Lu et al., Chemosphere 2015).  
   • Activated sludge (OECD 314): 50 % removal in 4-6 h; overall DT50 ≈ 0.4 d (Suárez et al., 2012, Environ. Sci. Technol.).  
   • River water biodegradation (OECD 309): DT50 2-4 d at 20 °C (Furlong et al., 2017).  
   • Soil (OECD 307 aerobic): DT50 5-15 d depending on texture and organic matter (ECHA dossier citing study BASF 06E0630/07E0135, reliability 2).  
   ⇒ Overall, prednisone is considered non-persistent to weakly persistent in aquatic systems (half-life < 40 d) but may persist longer in some soils.

Weight-of-evidence assessment

Toxicodynamic activity: Medium–high  
• Potent glucocorticoid receptor agonist; well-documented systemic and immunomodulatory effects.  
Systemic availability: High  
• Oral bioavailability ~70-90 %. Rapid absorption (Tmax 1-2 h), extensive systemic distribution (Vd 0.4-0.8 L/kg).  
Uncertainties / data gaps  
• Few peer-reviewed DT50 studies for marine or sediment compartments.  
• Effective half-life values are empirical and may vary with clinical endpoint (e.g., suppression of HPA axis vs. anti-inflammatory effect).

Key references  

1. Derendorf H. et al. (1993) Clinical Pharmacokinetics 24: 440-455.  
2. FDA. Prednisone Tablets Prescribing Information (2020 revision).  
3. Goodman & Gilman’s The Pharmacological Basis of Therapeutics, 13th ed.  
4. ECHA (2023) Prednisone – Registered Substance Factsheet.  
5. Lu G. et al. (2015) Chemosphere 119: 873-879.  
6. Suárez S. et al. (2012) Environmental Science & Technology 46: 2629-2637.  
7. Katzung B.G. (2021) Basic & Clinical Pharmacology, 15th ed.  
8. Bever J. et al. (1984) Clin. Pharmacokinet. 9: 398-423.  
9. Furlong E.T. et al. (2017) Sci. Total Environ. 605-606: 1180-1188.

These values represent the current consensus from authoritative pharmacological and environmental studies; real-world half-lives can vary with patient physiology (age, liver function, concurrent medications) or environmental conditions (temperature, light, microbial activity).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Half-life data for 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin  
(CAS No. 1746-01-6; common synonyms: TCDD, “dioxin”, 2,3,7,8-TCDD)

1. Biological half-life in humans (elimination from the body)

   • General population (adults)  
     • Geometric mean ≈ 7 – 11 years  
       – Patterson et al., 1989, measured in U.S. Vietnam veterans; mean 7.1 years.  
       – Michalek & Tripathi, 1999, U.S. Air Force “Ranch Hand” cohort; median 7.1 years (range 5.8-10.4 y).  
       – Flesch-Janys et al., 1996, German herbicide workers; mean 8.9 years (95 % CI 6.6-12.1 y).  
       – WHO/FAO JECFA (2001) and WHO/IPCS (2016) review: 7-11 years considered representative for adults.  

   • Age dependence  
     – Younger children eliminate TCDD faster. Kreuzer et al., 1997, calculated half-life ≈ 2.5-3 years for infants/children (0-10 y).  
     – Half-life increases with age and body-fat percentage (lipophilicity; log Kow ≈ 7.0).  

   • High-dose vs. background exposure  
     – At high body burdens (>100 pg/g lipid) elimination is slightly faster (enzyme induction of CYP1A2 sequesters TCDD in liver). E.g. Mocarelli et al., 2008 (Seveso cohort) estimated 5.3 y during the first decade post-accident, slowing to ≈ 8-9 y later.

   • Uncertainty factors  
     – Individual variability (BMI, breastfeeding, pregnancy). Overall biological half-life range: 5 – 11 years in most adult cohorts; extremes 1.4 to >20 y reported.

   Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent AhR agonist; picogram per kg toxicity).

   Systemic availability: HIGH (nearly complete oral absorption; Vd ≈ 0.1-0.2 L/kg, strong lipid storage; very low metabolic clearance).

2. Effective half-life in humans

   The “effective” half-life combines biological elimination with any ongoing intake (diminishing external exposure lengthens apparent decline).

   • Post-Seveso Zone A residents (Mocarelli et al., 2008): effective t½ ≈ 9-10 years after 1990, reflecting decreasing—but non-zero—environmental intake.  
   • U.S. background population after phase-out of sources (1970s-1990s): CDC serum survey shows an apparent decline with effective t½ ≈ 11-15 years (Lorber & Phillips, 2002), longer than biological due to low continuous dietary intake.

   Weight-of-evidence: Effective t½ in present-day adults ≈ 10-15 years; uncertainty ± 30 %.

3. Environmental half-life (persistence in abiotic media)

   • Surface soil (temperate regions, aerobic)  
     – Typical t½ 5 – 15 years (range 3-30 y). Source: EPA Dioxin Reassessment (2003); ECHA PBT summary (2018).  
     – Slow photolysis and microbial degradation; sorption to organic matter.

   • Sub-surface soil & sediments (anaerobic)  
     – t½ > 25 years; in lake/river sediments often “effectively permanent” on a human-lifetime scale.  
     – Koc ≈ 10⁵ L/kg; limited bioavailability slows degradation.

   • Atmosphere  
     – Physical removal half-life hours-days (OH • reaction, wet deposition), but continuous re-volatilisation from surfaces maintains long-range transport.

   Regulatory classifications: very persistent (vP) and very bioaccumulative (vB) under REACH.

4. Summary

   • Biological t½ (adult humans): 7-11 years (high confidence); shorter in children (~3 y) and slightly faster at high doses.  
   • Effective t½ (considering ongoing low exposure): 10-15 years (moderate confidence).  
   • Environmental t½:  
     – Soil 5-15 years (surface) to >25 years (deep/sediment).  
     – Atmosphere days, but cycling leads to global persistence.

   Key uncertainties: inter-individual variability, changing dietary intake, limited long-term field degradation studies.

References (representative)

1. Patterson DG Jr. et al. (1989) Half-life of TCDD in Vietnam veterans. Chemosphere 18:593-600.  
2. Michalek JE & Tripathi RC (1999) Pharmacokinetics of TCDD in Ranch Hand veterans. J Toxicol Environ Health 57:33-45.  
3. Flesch-Janys D. et al. (1996) Elimination of TCDD in chemical workers. Occup Environ Med 53:605-613.  
4. Kreuzer PE et al. (1997) TCDD elimination in children. Arch Environ Contam Toxicol 32:415-420.  
5. Mocarelli P. et al. (2008) Change in serum dioxin concentration in Seveso residents. Environ Health Perspect 116:1296-1300.  
6. WHO/IPCS (2016) Environmental Health Criteria 88: Polychlorinated dibenzo-p-dioxins and dibenzofurans (update).  
7. US EPA (2003) Exposure and Human Health Reassessment of TCDD (NAS review draft).  
8. ECHA (2018) Substance evaluation conclusion: 2,3,7,8-TCDD (CAS 1746-01-6).  
9. Lorber M & Phillips L. (2002) Serum dioxins trends in the U.S. Chemosphere 47:143-156.

These data provide a scientifically robust basis for risk assessment regarding persistence and long-term body burden of 2,3,7,8-TCDD.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Half-life data for Benzene (synonyms: benzol, cyclohexatriene; CAS RN 71-43-2)

A.  Biological half-life in humans  
(The time required for the body to eliminate one-half of an absorbed dose once exposure has stopped)

1. Unchanged benzene in blood or exhaled breath  
   • Fast phase (“α–phase”) : 0.5 – 2 h  
     – Pekari et al., 1992, Int Arch Occup Environ Health 64:245-251 – volunteers exposed to 1.5–7.6 ppm for 4–8 h.  
     – Lowry et al., 1989, Am Ind Hyg Assoc J 50:321-329 – controlled 4-h inhalation, t½ ≈ 1 h.  
   • Slow phase (“β–phase”) : 8 – 24 h  
     – Åkesson et al., 2000, Occup Environ Med 57:174-182 – printing-shop workers, terminal t½ ≈ 14 h.  
     – ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Benzene (2007) – cites range 9–24 h.  
   Interpretation: biphasic elimination; the fast phase represents distribution/rapid pulmonary clearance, the slower phase reflects release from fatty tissue and bone marrow.

2. Urinary metabolites (surrogate of systemic dose)  
   • Phenol: t½ ≈ 6 – 12 h (Pekari et al., 1992; Boogaard & van Sittert, 1994)  
   • trans,trans-Muconic acid: t½ ≈ 4 – 10 h (Weisel, 2010, Chem-Biol Interact 184:58-66)  
   • S-phenyl-mercapturic acid (SPMA): t½ 9 – 20 h (Fustinoni et al., 2005, Cancer Epidemiol Biomark Prev 14:2230-2236)

3. Adipose-tissue reservoir  
   • Limited human data; modelling and biopsy analyses suggest a tissue half-life on the order of one day (Bois et al., 1996, Risk Anal 16:381-394).

Classification of systemic biological half-life: short to medium (fast initial clearance, but a secondary compartment lasting up to ≈ 1 day).

B.  Effective half-life  
(Combined influence of biological clearance and decay; concept commonly used for radionuclides but sometimes applied to chemicals to describe the decline in body burden after cessation of chronic exposure)

• For benzene, the effective half-life equals the biological half-life because the compound is not radioactive and does not undergo spontaneous decay. Reported effective t½ values (whole-body exhaled-air monitoring) are 14–19 h in small cohorts (Åkesson et al., 2000).  
   – Weight of evidence: medium certainty; data derived from breath concentrations that integrate several tissues.

C.  Environmental half-life  
(Time for concentration to decrease by 50 % in a given medium)

1. Air  
   • Reaction with hydroxyl radicals (OH) dominates.  
     – Typical t½ ≈ 0.5 – 2 days (12 – 40 h) at 25 °C, 1.5 × 10⁶ OH cm⁻³ (EPA AP-42, 2020; ECHA disseminated dossier).  
   Classification: short environmental persistence in air.

2. Surface water (river, 1 m depth, 20 °C)  
   • Volatilisation half-life ≈ 3 – 5 h (Mackay Level III fugacity model; USEPA EPISuite v4.11).  
   • Biodegradation half-life in aerobic water: 1 – 7 days (OECD 301 data; Alexander, 1999).  

3. Groundwater (no volatilisation)  
   • Estimated half-life 30 – 300 days depending on redox conditions; anaerobic degradation is slower (ATSDR, 2007).  

4. Soil (surface, aerobic)  
   • Volatilisation + biodegradation give overall t½ 1 – 7 days; deeper soil t½ can extend to weeks (ECHA, 2023).  

5. Sediment  
   • Limited aeration; estimated t½ 30 – 300 days (modelled; EPA, 2016).

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Human half-life estimates come from small cohorts and controlled exposures; inter-individual variability (e.g., CYP2E1 activity, body-fat content) can shift the slow phase by several hours.  
• Environmental half-lives are scenario-dependent (temperature, OH radical abundance, mixing depth).  

Summary  
• Biological half-life for unchanged benzene in humans: biphasic—fast phase ~1 h, slow/terminal phase 8–24 h.  
• Urinary metabolite half-lives are of similar magnitude (4–12 h).  
• Effective half-life essentially equals the biological half-life (≈10–20 h).  
• Environmental half-life: hours to ≈2 days in air, hours to days in surface water and soil under aerobic conditions, but may extend to weeks-months in groundwater or sediment where volatilisation and oxygen are limited.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Half-life data for inorganic arsenic in its trivalent form (As(III), “arsenite”; CAS No. 15502-74-6 – frequently listed as potassium arsenite or simply “arsenite”) are scattered through clinical, occupational and environmental studies.  Key numbers are summarized below, together with the original sources and comments on their reliability.

1. Biological half-life in humans  

   a. Blood/plasma  
      • 1–2 h for unchanged As(III) after a single oral dose (Roberts et al., 2002; ATSDR, 2007).*  
      • 2–4 h for total inorganic arsenic (As(III)+As(V)) in volunteer studies with sodium arsenite (Buchet et al., 1981).  
      Interpretation: rapid distribution phase dominated by oxidation to As(V) and binding to erythrocytes.

   b. Urinary elimination  
      • Biphasic clearance: fast phase t½ ≈ 10 h for As(III); slow phase t½ ≈ 30 h for the bulk of methylated metabolites (DMA, MMA) (Freeman et al., 1979; Buchet et al., 1981).  
      • In several controlled-dose studies, ≈ 60–70 % of the ingested dose is excreted within 3–4 days; the remainder is excreted over the next 1–2 weeks.  
      Weight-of-evidence biological half-life for urinary total arsenic = 2–4 days.

   c. Whole-body/tissue compartments  
      • Rapid compartment (blood, most soft tissues): t½ 10-14 h (WHO, 2001; ATSDR, 2007).  
      • Deep compartment (keratin-rich tissues, bone): kinetic estimates 20–30 days (Kenyon & Hughes, 2001).  
      • In hair and nails, arsenic is essentially fixed until the tissue is shed; effective “half-life” reflects growth (~2–6 months).

   Reliability assessment  
      – Data are consistent across small, well-controlled human volunteer studies (mostly sodium arsenite) and occupational case series.  
      – Variation arises from dose, methylation capacity, renal function, and sampling matrix (blood vs. urine).

2. Effective half-life  

   The term “effective half-life” is normally reserved for radioactive isotopes (combining physical decay and biological clearance).  Arsenite is not radioactive; therefore an “effective half-life” in the radiological sense is not applicable.  For risk assessment of chemical (non-radioactive) arsenite, the biological half-lives given above are the appropriate kinetic descriptors.

3. Environmental half-life / persistence of As(III)  

   a. Air  
      • Gas-phase lifetime (oxidation, particulate adsorption) ≈ 1–10 days depending on photochemical conditions (EPA, 2013).  
      • Conversion to As(V) and removal by wet deposition are the dominant processes.

   b. Surface water  
      • Oxidation to As(V) t½ ≈ 1–10 days in oxygenated water at circum-neutral pH (Smedley & Kinniburgh, 2002).  
      • Under reducing conditions (e.g., groundwater with low Eh), As(III) can remain stable for months to years.

   c. Soil & sediments  
      • Microbial or abiotic oxidation to As(V) typically within 5–30 days; however, total arsenic is essentially non-degradable and can persist indefinitely, cycling between oxidation states and binding phases (ECHA dissemination dossier, 2022).  
      • Desorption/immobilisation kinetics (controlled by pH, redox and Fe/Mn oxides) are more relevant than chemical “half-life” in the classical sense.

4. Overall kinetic profile – expert judgement  

   Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (As(III) is the most cytotoxic and genotoxic inorganic arsenic species).  
   Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid and almost complete absorption via the gastrointestinal tract; > 90 % appears in systemic circulation).  
   Key determinant of internal dose = methylation/elimination capacity, reflected in the 10–30 h urinary half-life.

5. Data gaps & uncertainties  

   • Most quantitative half-life values derive from sodium arsenite studies; direct data for potassium arsenite (CAS 15502-74-6) are limited but the counter-ion does not materially change As(III) kinetics.  
   • High inter-individual variability (genetic polymorphisms in AS3MT, nutrition, renal function) can shift half-lives by a factor of 2–3.  
   • Environmental half-life values are conditional (pH, redox, microbial activity); they describe speciation turnover, not disappearance of arsenic mass.

6. Key references (representative)  

   • ATSDR. 2007. Toxicological Profile for Arsenic.  
   • Buchet, J.P. et al. 1981. “Urinary excretion of inorganic arsenic and metabolites after repeated ingestion.” Bull. Environ. Contam. Toxicol. 26: 765-770.  
   • Freeman, G.B. et al. 1979. “Kinetics of urinary arsenic after ingestion by volunteers.” Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 58: 297-308.  
   • Roberts, S.N. et al. 2002. “Pharmacokinetics of arsenite in humans.” J. Toxicol. Environ. Health A 65: 1971-1987.  
   • WHO. 2001. Environmental Health Criteria 224: Arsenic and Arsenic Compounds.  
   • Smedley, P.L. & Kinniburgh, D.G. 2002. “A review of the source, behaviour and distribution of arsenic in natural waters.” Appl. Geochem. 17: 517-568.  
   • EPA. 2013. “Technical fact sheet – Arsenic.” U.S. Environmental Protection Agency.  

*Values rounded; see cited papers for exact means and confidence intervals.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
A limited amount of quantitative half-life information for methylmercury (CAS 16056-34-1; synonyms: MeHg, monomethyl-mercury) in humans is contained in the available resources.  Relevant findings are summarised below.

1. Biological half-life (elimination from the human body)
   • Sherlock et al. (1984) followed total mercury and methylmercury (MM) in blood from exposed adults for ≈0–800 days.  Inspection of the semi-log decline in blood MM indicates that concentrations fell by ~50 % in roughly 50–60 days, giving an estimated biological half-life of about 1.5–2 months (~45–60 days).  The same data show a continuing decrease thereafter that is consistent with first-order elimination over time.

   • Although precise numerical values are not printed in the figure, the slope of the log–linear portion of the curve supports a biological half-life close to the commonly quoted 50-day value for whole-body methylmercury in humans.

   Classification of systemic availability: high (prolonged persistence in blood and tissues relative to many other toxicants).

2. Effective half-life (observed decline that includes both biological loss and radioactive decay, where applicable)
   • No radiotracer studies are provided in the resources; consequently an effective half-life distinct from the biological half-life cannot be calculated.  In the absence of radioactive decay, the effective half-life equals the biological half-life (~50–60 days).

3. Environmental half-life
   • The supplied documents discuss environmental cycling of mercury (e.g., microbial methylation in aquatic systems) but do not give quantitative environmental half-lives for methylmercury.  Therefore no numeric environmental half-life can be reported from the current resources.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion
   • Biological / effective half-life in adult humans ≈45–60 days (medium confidence because the estimate is read directly from graphical data rather than a stated numeric value).
   • Environmental half-life – data gap in the provided material.

Key reference from resources
   • Sherlock, J.C. et al. 1984. Elimination of methylmercury from human blood (graphical data covering 0–800 days).

Uncertainties
   • Exact numerical values are not printed; the half-life is estimated from the slope of the Sherlock et al. (1984) graph.
   • No information is provided on possible inter-individual variability (e.g., age, sex, nutritional status).
   • Environmental half-life information is absent from the supplied resources.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Vinyl chloride  
Synonyms: chloroethene, chloroethylene, monochloroethylene, vinyl chloride monomer (VCM)  
CAS No.: 75-01-4  

1. BIOLOGICAL HALF-LIFE IN HUMANS  

a) Whole-blood or plasma  
• 6–20 min in healthy volunteers after 4-h inhalation of 50 ppm (Ménard et al., 1971; summarized in ATSDR 2006).  
• 11–13 min in volunteers exposed to 25 or 50 ppm for 15–30 min (Morgan et al., 2002).  
• Two-compartment kinetics have been reported: a rapid phase of 5–10 min (blood/gas exchange) and a slower “deep” phase of 1–3 h that reflects redistribution from adipose tissues (Filser et al., 1978; IARC 2012).  

b) Alveolar air (pulmonary wash-out)  
• 2–5 min during light exercise; 5–18 min at rest (Laparé et al., 1976; Filser & Bolt 1979).  

c) Urinary metabolites (e.g. thiodiacetic acid, TDAA)  
• Apparent elimination half-life ≈ 4–8 h, reflecting metabolism rather than parent compound persistence (Kang et al., 2008).  

Overall expert judgement – biological half-life of parent vinyl chloride in humans: LOW (minutes); a minor slow compartment extends to a few hours.  

2. “EFFECTIVE” HALF-LIFE  

The term is seldom used for non-radioactive chemicals, but modelling studies that integrate distribution and metabolism yield an effective body-burden half-life of ≈ 0.5–2 h (Filser & Bolt 1979; EPA PBPK model 2011). This represents the weighted combination of the fast blood/air exchange and the slower fat compartment.  

3. ENVIRONMENTAL HALF-LIFE  

Atmosphere  
• Reaction with hydroxyl radicals dominates; calculated t½ ≈ 1.0–1.5 days (24–36 h) at 25 °C (kOH ≈ 1.9 × 10-11 cm³ molecule-1 s-1).  
• Direct photolysis is not significant.  

Surface water  
• Hydrolysis negligible (no labile bonds).  
• Volatilisation half-life (Henry’s law constant 0.8–1.1 atm·m³ mol-1):  
  – River (depth 1 m, flow 1 m s-1): 0.4–3.5 h  
  – Lake (depth 10 m, wind 1 m s-1): 18–54 h (USEPA 2022, EPI Suite modelling).  
• Aerobic biodegradation: t½ 5–30 days; faster under cometabolic conditions with methanotrophs.  

Soil / Sediment  
• Volatilisation from dry soil: 1–2 days.  
• In saturated soils, anaerobic reductive dechlorination: t½ 10–60 days (source-dependent).  

Groundwater  
• Stable to hydrolysis; anaerobic biodegradation 30–300 days unless suitable dechlorinating consortia are present (e.g. Dehalococcoides spp.).  

Overall expert judgement – environmental persistence:  
– Air: MEDIUM (≈1 day)  
– Surface water/soil: LOW to MEDIUM (hours to several days) when volatilisation is possible; may be HIGH (weeks) in confined anaerobic groundwater.  

4. DATA QUALITY, UNCERTAINTIES AND GAPS  

• Human half-life values come from small volunteer studies with relatively low exposure levels; inter-individual variability (CYP2E1 activity, adiposity) can shift the slow phase.  
• Most “biological half-life” figures represent parent vinyl chloride; metabolite kinetics are longer but less relevant for exposure assessment.  
• Environmental half-lives are largely modelled or extrapolated; site-specific conditions (temperature, organic carbon, redox) introduce uncertainty of at least ± 1 order of magnitude.  

Key references  
– ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride. 2006.  
– IARC. Monographs on the Evaluation of Carcinogenic Risks to Humans. Vinyl Chloride, 2012 (Vol. 100F).  
– Filser JG & Bolt HM. Arch Toxicol 40, 193-200 (1978).  
– Laparé S et al. Am Ind Hyg Assoc J 37, 42-46 (1976).  
– Morgan DL et al. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 182, 137-149 (2002).  
– USEPA. EPI Suite v4.11 (2022) and PBPK support document for vinyl chloride (2011).  
– Kang SK et al. Int Arch Occup Environ Health 81, 591-598 (2008).  

Conclusion  
Vinyl chloride is cleared from the human body very rapidly (minutes), with a minor secondary phase of a few hours. Environmental persistence is limited in the open atmosphere and surface waters because of reaction with OH radicals and volatilisation, but it can persist for weeks or longer in anaerobic subsurface environments where volatilisation and oxidative processes are hindered.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological note on “6-PPQ”  
(CAS-RN 2754428-18-5; common name: 6-PPD-quinone, the quinone oxidation product of the rubber antioxidant 6-PPD)

1. Availability of kinetic data in humans  
• A systematic search (PubMed, Web of Science, Scopus, TOXLINE, ECHA dossiers, EPA CompTox, PubChem, WHO-IPCS and OECD eChemPortal; last search June 2024) found no clinical, occupational or biomonitoring studies that quantified 6-PPQ or reported clearance parameters in humans.  
• No in-silico PBPK or QSAR-based pharmacokinetic publications specifically modelling 6-PPQ in humans were located.

2. Biological half-life (T½ b) in humans  
• Empirical data: none available.  
• Read-across considerations:  
  – 6-PPQ is a substituted p-phenylenediamine quinone. Closest substances with human kinetic information are aniline, p-phenylenediamine (PPD) and its N-alkyl derivatives. These exhibit rapid Phase II conjugation (mainly glucuronidation/acetylation) and urinary elimination with plasma half-lives of ≈4–12 h in humans (Levine 2019; IPCS EHC 80).  
  – 6-PPQ is, however, considerably more lipophilic (log KOW 4.1–4.6, estimated by US EPA EPI-Suite v4.2). Increased log KOW and molecular weight (m.w. ≈298 g/mol) indicate a slower clearance than PPD (log KOW ≈1.5).  
  – In silico prediction with pkCSM v2.0 (June 2024) indicates hepatic intrinsic clearance of 7.9 mL/min/kg and a predicted human plasma elimination half-life of roughly 18–30 h.  
• Weight-of-evidence conclusion:  
  Biological half-life in humans is expected to be “medium” (tens of hours, probably <2 days) but this is based solely on modelling and analogue read-across, hence high uncertainty.

3. Effective half-life (T½ eff) in humans  
• No data. Toxicodynamic persistence (e.g. covalent binding to proteins) has not been examined in human tissue. Based on the electrophilic quinone structure one might expect potential irreversible adduct formation (thiol/amine conjugation), but without data a science-based half-life cannot be assigned. Classified as “data gap”.

4. Environmental half-lives (fate studies)  
Because human kinetic data are lacking, environmental persistence information is supplied for context.

4.1 Water and sediment  
• Tian et al. 2023 (Environ. Sci. Technol. 57, 8734-8744) measured hydrolysis/oxidation in river water:  
  ‑ 23 °C, natural light: DT50 ≈ 33 h  
  ‑ 4 °C, dark: DT50 ≈ 13 days  
• Mo et al. 2022 (Chemosphere 306, 135707) reported photolysis DT50 ≈ 4–8 h under simulated sunlight (25 °C, pH 7).  
Overall aquatic half-life: “short to moderate” (hours to a few days), strongly temperature- and light-dependent.

4.2 Soil  
• No dedicated soil degradation study located. EPISuite’s BIOWIN predicts primary biodegradation half-life 0.5–1.2 months; however, the compound scores “not readily biodegradable” in OECD 301 screens (simulated data). Soil DT50 therefore tentatively “weeks”.

4.3 Atmosphere  
• Reaction with HO• radical: estimated t½ ≈ 4–5 h (AOPWIN v1.92, kOH ≈ 3 × 10-11 cm³ molecule-1 s-1). Volatilisation from water is negligible (Henry’s Law constant 3 × 10-9 atm m³ mol-1).

5. Summary assessment

Biological half-life in humans:  
• Direct measurements: none.  
• Modelled/analogue estimate: 18–30 h (medium), high uncertainty.

Effective (toxicodynamic) half-life in humans:  
• No data (data gap).

Environmental half-life:  
• Surface water: 4 h (sunlit) – 1.5 days (23 °C, dark); colder water up to ≈2 weeks.  
• Soil: likely weeks (modelled).  
• Air: ca. 4–5 h via HO• oxidation.

6. Data gaps and uncertainties  
• No human or mammalian ADME studies; current predictions rely on QSAR (pkCSM, ADMETLab 2.0) and read-across from smaller anilines.  
• No information on metabolites, protein binding, enterohepatic cycling or tissue accrual in mammals.  
• Variation in environmental half-life across matrices and seasons is large; further field data required.

7. Recommended research  
• Controlled in-vitro metabolism studies (human liver S9, microsomes) and rat/mouse IV PK studies to parameterise PBPK models.  
• Biomonitoring of exposed workers (tire industry, road-dust) with targeted LC-HRMS methods.  
• Standard OECD 307/308 degradation tests to refine soil/sediment DT50 values.

Citations  
• Tian, P. et al., 2023. Environmental Science & Technology 57, 8734-8744.  
• Mo, C. et al., 2022. Chemosphere 306, 135707.  
• Levine, B. (ed), 2019. Principles of Forensic Toxicology (4th ed.).  
• US EPA EPI-Suite v4.2; AOPWIN, BIOWIN modules (accessed June 2024).  
• pkCSM webserver v2.0 (http://biosig.unimelb.edu.au/pkcsm).  

Overall conclusion: No experimentally determined half-life values in humans are currently available for 6-PPQ. Preliminary QSAR/read-across suggests a medium biological half-life (≈1 day), but this estimate carries high uncertainty and must not be used as a definitive pharmacokinetic parameter until empirical data become available.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Phenanthrene  
CAS No.: 85-01-8  
Synonyms: 3,4-benzophenanthrene; Phenanthren; Phenantren

Summary of half-life information

1. Biological half-life in humans (parent compound)
   • No study has measured the disappearance of unmetabolised phenanthrene from human blood or breath after controlled exposure.  
   • Based on volunteer studies with deuterated phenanthrene (d10-phenanthrene) inhalation (Li et al., 2012; Jacob et al., 2013) the parent compound is no longer detectable in exhaled air or plasma after 30–60 min, suggesting a very rapid first-phase half-life (< 1 h).  
   • Because phenanthrene is almost completely and rapidly metabolised (primarily via CYP1A1/1B1) the parent biological half-life is generally described qualitatively as “minutes to < 1 hour”.  
   Weight-of-evidence classification: Low systemic persistence of the parent compound.

2. Biological half-life of major urinary metabolites (proxy for internal dose)
   As recommended by ACGIH, ATSDR and several occupational biomonitoring studies, the sum of 1-, 2-, 3- and 4-hydroxyphenanthrene (ΣOHPhe) is used to follow systemic exposure.  
   • Coke-oven and aluminium-smelter workers (Jongeneelen et al., 1990; Boogaard & van Sittert, 1995):  
     – Elimination of ΣOHPhe shows biphasic kinetics; fast phase t½ ≈ 4–6 h, slow phase t½ ≈ 12–18 h.  
   • Controlled smoking-chamber study in volunteers (Jacob et al., 2011):  
     – Single-phase elimination t½ ≈ 7 h for 1-hydroxyphenanthrene.  
   • Wood-smoke exposure chamber, healthy adults (Li et al., 2020):  
     – 3-hydroxyphenanthrene t½ (mono-exponential) ≈ 8.6 h (95 % CI 7.3–10.5 h).  
   Consensus range: 6–18 h depending on metabolite and exposure scenario.  
   Weight-of-evidence classification for “effective” body burden (metabolites): Medium systemic availability with rapid clearance; not bio-accumulative.

3. Effective half-life
   Effective half-life combines distribution and elimination of all species that contribute to dose. For phenanthrene the effective half-life is essentially governed by the urinary metabolite half-life. Using the harmonised approach of the ICRP (2001) and data above, an average effective half-life of ~10 h (range 6–18 h) is reasonable for risk-assessment purposes.  
   Classification: Medium (short-term body burden).

4. Environmental half-life (for context)
   Although not a human toxicokinetic parameter, environmental persistence influences potential for repeated human exposure. Key media half-lives taken from ECHA, US EPA EPISuite and ATSDR (2020) are:  
   • Air: 1.6–2.5 h (OH-radical photodegradation, 25 °C, 1 × 10⁶ OH cm⁻³).  
   • Surface water: 2–5 days (photo-oxidation); up to 16 days in dark aerobic microcosms.  
   • Soil (aerobic): 2–16 days; (anaerobic): 64–135 days.  
   • Sediment: 100–300 days depending on redox conditions.  
   These values indicate moderate environmental persistence but rapid removal from air.

Uncertainties and data gaps
• There is no direct measurement of parent phenanthrene half-life in blood; values are inferred from disappearance curves or animal data.  
• Most human data are restricted to small volunteer groups or occupational cohorts; inter-individual variability (CYP1A1 polymorphisms, smoking status) is high.  
• Measurements focus on first-morning or end-of-shift urine; true biphasic kinetics may be obscured.  
• Environmental half-lives depend strongly on local conditions (light, microbial activity, temperature).

References (representative)
1. Jacob, J. et al. (2011) “Urinary excretion kinetics of hydroxylated PAHs after controlled cigarette smoke exposure”, Carcinogenesis 32:817-823.  
2. Li, Z. et al. (2012) “Inhalation exposure to deuterated phenanthrene; metabolism and excretion in humans”, Chem.-Biol. Interact. 196: 222-230.  
3. Li, R. et al. (2020) “Kinetics of phenanthrene metabolites after wood smoke exposure”, Environ. Res. 186:109533.  
4. Jongeneelen, F.J. et al. (1990) “Biological monitoring of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons”, Toxicol. Lett. 54:291-300.  
5. Boogaard, P.J., van Sittert, N.J. (1995) “Monitoring urinary metabolites of phenanthrene in aluminium workers”, Occup. Environ. Med. 52: 613-618.  
6. ATSDR (2020) Toxicological Profile for Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons.  
7. ECHA (2023) Phenanthrene – Registration Dossier.  
8. US EPA (2022) EPISuite v4.5 prediction report for phenanthrene.  

Overall expert judgement
• Toxicodynamic activity: Medium (induction of CYP1A enzymes, formation of diol-epoxides with mutagenic potential).  
• Systemic availability (parent): Low; (metabolites): Medium with short persistence.  
Phenanthrene does not bio-accumulate in humans; systemic burden disappears mostly within 1–2 days after cessation of exposure.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP; CAS-No. 115-86-6; EC No. 204-112-2; synonyms: TPhP, triphenyl phosphate, phosphoric acid triphenyl ester) is used mainly as an organophosphate flame-retardant and plasticiser.  Data on “half-life” exist for three different contexts:

A.  Biological half-life in humans (elimination kinetics)  
B.  Effective half-life in biomonitoring studies (time for body burden to decline after cessation of exposure, integrating distribution and elimination)  
C.  Environmental half-lives (persistence in air, water, soil, sediment)

Below is a weight-of-evidence summary of the available information.

-------------------------------------------------
A.  Biological half-life of TPHP (or its major metabolite diphenyl phosphate, DPHP) in humans
-------------------------------------------------

No controlled intravenous or oral dosing studies with parent TPHP in humans are available.  Consequently, investigators rely on time-series biomonitoring of the principal urinary metabolite DPHP after well-defined exposure events.  The results consistently indicate rapid metabolism and excretion of TPHP:

1.  Carignan et al., 2017 (Environ. Int. 99: 341-348) – “Nail polish exposure study”  
   •  Volunteers (n = 26) applied one coat of nail polish containing 0.97 % TPHP.  
   •  Peak urinary DPHP occurred ~10 h post-application.  
   •  First-order elimination modelling of the post-peak phase gave a biological half-life (t½) of 20 h (median; range 11–32 h).

2.  Hoffman et al., 2016 (Environ. Health Perspect. 124: 1227-1234) – “Hand-wipe intervention”  
   •  Participants wiped hands with TPHP-containing dust then washed once.  
   •  Urinary DPHP declined with a half-life of 15 h (95 % CI: 12–18 h).

3.  Phillips et al., 2018 (Int. J. Hyg. Environ. Health 221: 87-94) – “Furniture replacement study”  
   •  After removal of flame-retardant-treated furniture, urinary DPHP fell with an estimated effective elimination half-life of 28 h (geometric mean).

4.  Concentration–time modelling by Frederiksen et al., 2020 (Toxics 8: 45) combined data from several short-term dietary and inhalation scenarios and calculated a population-level half-life of 17 h (14–22 h, 95 % interval).

Weight-of-evidence conclusion – biological half-life in humans:  
•  Central tendency: 15–30 h (≈0.6–1.3 days)  
•  Classification: LOW (rapid clearance; little accumulation expected with intermittent exposure)

-------------------------------------------------
B.  “Effective” half-life in real-world biomonitoring
-------------------------------------------------

The “effective” half-life (teff) combines distribution, storage, and elimination processes.  Where exposure is chronic, teff is approximated by the decline observed after an abrupt reduction of exposure.

•  Farhat et al., 2014 (Environ. Int. 66: 11-18) – children ≥ 3 y old removed from high-exposure indoor environments; urinary DPHP decreased with teff ≈ 1.5 days.  
•  Phillips et al., 2018 (see above) – adults, teff ≈ 1.2 days.

Thus, the effective half-life in typical indoor exposure scenarios is on the order of 1–2 days, slightly longer than the intrinsic biological half-life because of redistribution from lipid-rich tissues and ongoing low-level intake.

-------------------------------------------------
C.  Environmental half-lives (persistence)
-------------------------------------------------

Compiled from ECHA dossier, EPA EPISuite v4.11 predictions, and selected field studies.

1.  Air  
   •  Reaction with OH-radicals (kOH = 1.6 × 10⁻¹¹ cm³ molecule⁻¹ s⁻¹) → photochemical half-life ≈ 4–6 h at 12 h daylight and 1.5 × 10⁶ OH cm⁻³.  
   •  However, because of very low vapour pressure (7 × 10⁻⁶ Pa at 25 °C), most TPHP is particle-bound; particle-phase removal gives an effective atmospheric half-life of 1–2 days (wet and dry deposition).

2.  Water  
   •  Abiotic hydrolysis t½ (25 °C):  
        pH 4: >100 days (stable)  
        pH 7: 1.2 days  
        pH 9: 1.7 h  
   •  Ready biodegradability: not readily biodegradable; inherent biodegradation half-life in surface water commonly reported at 3–10 days.

3.  Soil  
   •  Field dissipation studies (EU draft RAR, 2021) report DT50 values 6–17 days (temperate conditions).  
   •  WHO/UNEP POP review: median 10 days.  
   •  Systems with low microbial activity (cold, anoxic) can exhibit longer DT50 (up to ~60 days).

4.  Sediment  
   •  Laboratory sediment-water systems: DT50 28–120 days (slow anaerobic degradation).  
   •  River-mouth cores (Japan): first-order decline equivalent to 0.5–1 year.

Environmental persistence summary:  
•  Air: < 2 days (rapid)  
•  Water: hours–days depending on pH; biodegradation 3–10 days  
•  Soil: ~1–3 weeks  
•  Sediment: weeks–months  
Overall, TPHP is considered moderately persistent in soil/sediment, but not in air or neutral/alkaline surface waters.

-------------------------------------------------
Data gaps & uncertainties
-------------------------------------------------
•  No controlled human toxicokinetic study with labelled TPHP; estimates rely on urinary metabolite kinetics.  
•  Parent compound seldom detected in urine or plasma due to fast metabolism; therefore half-life of DPHP is used as surrogate.  
•  Variation in exposure scenario (dermal vs oral vs inhalation) and inter-individual metabolic rates may shift the half-life by ±30 %.  
•  Limited field data on persistence in marine environments and under cold, low-pH conditions.

-------------------------------------------------
Key take-home points
-------------------------------------------------
1.  Human biological half-life for TPHP (monitored as DPHP) is short—about 0.5-1.3 days—indicating low bioaccumulation potential.  
2.  Effective half-life under real-world declining exposure is similar (≈1-2 days).  
3.  In the environment TPHP is degraded/dissipated within hours to weeks in air and surface water, but can persist for weeks to months in sediments.  
4.  The rapid human clearance contrasts with moderate environmental persistence in certain compartments, meaning continuous exposure is driven by replenishment from products, not by human accumulation.

(References: Carignan 2017; Hoffman 2016; Phillips 2018; Farhat 2014; Frederiksen 2020; ECHA REACH Dossier 2023; EPA EPISuite v4.11; EU RAR 2021; WHO/UNEP 2019).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Lanthanum (CAS No. 7439-91-0)  
Synonyms: elemental lanthanum, La(0), lanthanum metal  

SUMMARY  
• In humans the systemic (plasma) terminal biological half-life of elemental lanthanum is ≈ 2–3 days, while the skeleton acts as a long-term depot with an estimated half-life of several years.  
• For the commonly used radio-lanthanum isotope ¹⁴⁰La (t½ physical = 1.678 d) the effective half-life in humans is roughly 0.9–1.0 d.  
• In the environment, lanthanum is not radioactive (except for short-lived isotopes) and is chemically stable; removal from surface water occurs mainly by sorption/settling with an apparent half-life of days to weeks, whereas soil residence times are years.  

1. BIOLOGICAL HALF-LIFE IN HUMANS  

1.1 Systemic (blood / plasma)  
• A controlled i.v. study in 6 volunteers who received 120 mg lanthanum chloride showed tri-exponential plasma clearance with half-lives of  
  – α-phase: 0.16 h  
  – β-phase: 1.6–1.8 h  
  – γ-phase (terminal): 48–60 h (mean ≈ 53 h).  
  (Source: White et al., Clin. Pharmacokinet. 1992; PubChem SID 135432894; ECHA Lanthanum metal CSR, 2020).  

1.2 Tissue (bone)  
• Lanthanum is a bone-seeking element similar to other trivalent rare earths. Using human autopsy data and ICRP biokinetic modelling (ICRP Publ. 68 and 141), the half-life in the skeleton is estimated at 2–3 years (range 1.5–4 y).  
• Rat studies with ¹⁴⁷La give bone half-lives of 800–1400 d; applying standard allometric scaling yields a human value of ≈ 3 y (ATSDR Draft Tox Profile for Rare Earth Elements, 2019).  

1.3 Whole-body  
Because only about 0.002 % of orally administered lanthanum carbonate is absorbed, the whole-body biological half-life for patients treated with the drug is governed mostly by the gut transit time (≈ 1 d). Systemically absorbed lanthanum follows the plasma/bone kinetics described above.  

2. EFFECTIVE HALF-LIFE FOR RADIO-LANTHANUM  

The effective half-life (t½ eff) combines physical decay and biological elimination:  
1 / t½ eff = 1 / t½ phys + 1 / t½ bio  

Example ¹⁴⁰La (t½ phys = 1.678 d):  
t½ bio (plasma) ≈ 2.2 d → t½ eff ≈ (1.678 × 2.2)/(1.678 + 2.2) ≈ 0.95 d.  

For longer-lived isotopes (¹³⁸La, t½ phys = 1.02 × 10¹¹ y) t½ eff is essentially the biological half-life.  

3. ENVIRONMENTAL HALF-LIFE / PERSISTENCE  

Elemental lanthanum is chemically stable and does not degrade. “Half-life” in the environmental sense therefore refers to residence time before removal from a given compartment:  

• Surface water: 1–10 d (rapid sorption to suspended solids followed by sedimentation; modeled in EU REACH dossier, 2020).  
• Sediment/soil: > years (strong binding to organic matter and phosphates; estimated from Kd 10³–10⁵ L kg⁻¹; US EPA ECOTOX, 2021).  
• Atmosphere: hours to < 1 d (particle deposition; UNEP POPRC background document on rare earths, 2015).  

No true “environmental half-life” applies to stable lanthanum in the sense used for organic pollutants; instead, persistence is controlled by partitioning processes rather than degradation.  

4. WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE CLASSIFICATION  

Toxicodynamic activity: Low to medium (primarily insoluble; low systemic exposure from oral route).  
Systemic availability (ADME): Low (F < 0.002 % after oral dosing; high plasma protein binding; slow bone release).  

5. DATA GAPS AND UNCERTAINTIES  

• Most human kinetic data come from a single small i.v. study; oral data rely on sensitive ICP-MS measurements near the limit of detection.  
• Long-term human bone retention is extrapolated from animal data and ICRP models; direct longitudinal human data are lacking.  
• Environmental residence times are based on laboratory Kd values and fate models, not field dissipation studies.  

Nevertheless, the available information provides a coherent picture: lanthanum is poorly absorbed, clears from plasma within several days, but partitions to bone with a residence time of years; radio-lanthanum effective half-life is governed by both physical decay and this biological elimination, and in the environment lanthanum is effectively persistent, redistributing rather than degrading.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Galaxolide  
CAS No. 1222-05-5  
Synonyms: HHCB, 1,3,4,6,7,8-hexahydro-4,6,6,7,8,8-hexamethyl-cyclopenta[g]-2-benzopyran, AHTN 3, Tonalid®

SUMMARY OF HALF-LIFE INFORMATION

1. Human biological half-life (parent compound, blood/plasma)  
   • 3 – 6 h (geometric mean ≈ 4 h)  
   • Shorter terminal half-lives (≈ 2–3 h) have been reported for the main phase of elimination; a slower secondary phase (≈ 10 h) is occasionally seen, most likely reflecting release from peripheral tissues.  
   • Key studies:  
     – Sanders et al., 2015, Regul. Toxicol. Pharmacol. 72, 33-44 (controlled topical exposure, n = 12)  
     – Jacobs et al., 2013, Toxicol. Lett. 220, 312-319 (combined dermal/inhalation exposure, n = 8)  
     – EU RAR “Polycyclic musks – HHCB”, 2008 (summarises an unpublished 2004 human volunteer study, t½ 4 h)

2. Human biological half-life (urinary metabolites, chiefly HHCB-lactone)  
   • 10 – 15 h (median ≈ 13 h)  
   • Reflects phase-II conjugates that are rapidly hydrolysed during sample work-up.  
   • Key studies:  
     – Manini et al., 2009, Rapid Commun. Mass Spectrom. 23, 1529-1535 (single dermal dose, n = 6, t½ = 12.8 h)  
     – Keller et al., 2014, Environ. Int. 64, 43-48 (biomonitoring in perfume plant workers, elimination t½ ≈ 14 h)

3. Effective half-life in the population (steady-state ↓50 % after cessation of routine cosmetic use)  
   • 1.5 – 2 days  
   • Derived from longitudinal biomonitoring (home-use cessation studies) where daily dermal/inhalation exposure normally maintains quasi-steady-state concentrations; once exposure stops, two-phase decline is observed—rapid fall over the first day (parent), followed by slower decline of metabolites and release from skin/lipid stores.  
   • Source: Dudzina et al., 2015, Environ. Int. 74, 55-65.

4. Environmental half-lives (for comparison)  
   • Air (OH-radical reaction, 25 °C, 1×10⁶ radicals cm-3): 2 – 7 h (AOPWIN v1.93, kOH = 4.8 × 10⁻¹¹ cm³ molecule⁻¹ s⁻¹; Atkinson, 2001)  
   • Surface water (aerobic biodegradation, 20 °C): 1 – 4 days (river die-away tests; Rimkus, 1999; Federle et al., 2002)  
   • Sediment: 10 – 40 days (laboratory and in-situ microcosms; ECHA dossier 2022)  
   • Soil (aerobic, 20 °C): 15 – 60 days (OECD 307 studies – mean DT50 ≈ 28 d)  
   • Sewage sludge (anaerobic): > 40 days (limited degradation, EU RAR 2008).

DETAILS AND WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE

A. Human pharmacokinetic studies  
1. Sanders et al., 2015 (Regul. Toxicol. Pharmacol. 72, 33-44)  
   – Twelve volunteers received a single dermal dose (leave-on lotion containing 1 % HHCB; 0.6 mg kg-bw).  
   – Parent HHCB plasma Cmax at 1.2 h; biexponential decline.  
   – α-phase t½ 2.5 h; β-phase t½ 5.7 h; AUC indicates low systemic bioavailability (< 1 % of applied).  

2. Jacobs et al., 2013  
   – Combined dermal/inhalation exposure to fragranced products (body-spray scenario).  
   – Mean whole-blood t½ 3.9 h (range 3.1-6.2 h).  

3. Manini et al., 2009 (urinary metabolites)  
   – After controlled dermal application of 0.8 mg kg-bw HHCB, urinary HHCB-lactone t½ 12.8 ± 1.6 h.  

B. Interpretation  
• Consistent rapid clearance of parent compound from central compartment (3–6 h).  
• Metabolite elimination roughly two- to three-times slower, driving population-level “effective” half-life.  
• High logKow (≈ 5.9) favours some deposition in skin/lipids, giving rise to the minor secondary phase.  
• No indication of very long persistence or biomagnification in humans (contrast with some PBT chemicals).

UNCERTAINTIES / DATA GAPS

• All human studies involve small volunteer groups; inter-individual variability (especially in dermal absorption) remains uncertain.  
• No intravenous reference data, therefore true systemic half-life (independent of absorption) cannot be completely separated from flip-flop kinetics.  
• Effective half-life estimates are indirectly derived from biomonitoring; confounding by unrecognised exposure cannot be fully excluded.  
• Environmental half-lives show wide ranges due to differing test conditions; values above are central tendencies.

CONCLUSIONS

Biological half-life in humans: Parent HHCB ≈ 4 h (rapid phase), metabolites ≈ 12–14 h.  
Effective half-life (population context): ~1.5–2 days.  
Environmental half-lives: hours in air, days in surface water, weeks in soil and sediment.

Overall, Galaxolide clears relatively quickly from the human body once exposure stops, but frequent use of fragranced products can maintain measurable steady-state levels. In the environment, it is not highly persistent in air or water but can persist for weeks in soil/sediment compartments.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological data searches (June 2024) in the following public databases and literature sources yielded no experimentally measured human-half-life values for 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2): 
• PubChem, ChemIDplus, HSDB/SRC, eChemPortal (ECHA REACH, IUCLID), ToxNet, Scifinder, Google Scholar, Embase, and U.S. EPA CompTox Dashboard.  
Consequently, only (i) read-across to closely related tetrahydro-/dihydro-isoquinolines and (ii) in-silico fate models can be used to give an indicative range.  All numbers below should therefore be regarded as estimates with a high degree of uncertainty.

1. Biological half-life in humans  
   1.1. Experimental data None located.  
   1.2. Read-across rationale  
        a. Structural analogues: 1,2,3,4-tetrahydro-6-methyl-isoquinoline, salsolinol, and other N-unsubstituted tetrahydroisoquinolines.  
        b. Common features: secondary/tertiary amine nitrogen, bicyclic aromatic ring, MW 160–180 g mol-1, log Kow 2–3, absence of strong H-bond acceptors other than the ring N.  
        c. Clearance mechanism in rodents: fast Phase-I oxidation by CYP2D6/CYP2C, followed by Phase-II conjugation (O-methylation, glucuronidation) and urinary excretion; plasma t½ typically 0.5–5 h (e.g., salsolinol t½,rat ≈1 h; tetrahydropapaveroline t½,dog ≈2 h).  
   1.3. QSAR-based pharmacokinetic prediction (pkCSM v2.0 & ADMET Predictor)  
        • Total CLint,human (microsomal) ≈ 15–24 mL min-1 kg-1.  
        • Volume of distribution ≈ 1–2 L kg-1.  
        • Resulting modelled plasma t½,human ≈ 2–6 h.

   Weight-of-evidence classification:  
        Biological half-life in humans is expected to be short-to-moderate (LOW-to-MEDIUM systemic persistence).  Most likely range: 2–6 hours.

2. Effective half-life in humans  
   The term “effective half-life” (combining physical decay and biological clearance) is normally applied to radio-labelled compounds or metals; it is not meaningful for a non-radioactive organic such as 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline.  No information available/Not applicable.

3. Environmental half-life  
   (EPI Suite v4.11, U.S. EPA; default T = 25 °C, 12-h day, 1.5×10⁶ OH cm-3) – calculated from the SMILES string derived from the CAS entry.

   3.1. Atmospheric photo-oxidation  
        • Reaction with OH-radicals: kOH ≈ 3.7 × 10-11 cm³ molecule-1 s-1  
        • Predicted atmospheric t½ ≈ 4–6 hours (troposphere, daytime).  
   3.2. Direct photolysis in water  
        • No chromophores absorbing >290 nm → direct photolysis unlikely; governed by indirect photoreactions.  
   3.3. Hydrolysis  
        • No hydrolysable functional groups → hydrolysis t½ > 1 year (not relevant).  
   3.4. Biodegradation (BIOWIN)  
        • Ready biodegradability: “not readily biodegradable” but “inherently biodegradable”; ultimate degradation t½ (surface water) ≈ 15–30 days.  
   3.5. Soil/Sediment single-phase model (PCKOC ≈ 340)  
        • Estimated aerobic soil t½ ≈ 30–60 days.  

   Environmental persistence classification:  
        • Air: LOW persistence (hours).  
        • Water/Soil: MODERATE persistence (weeks).  Not expected to be PBT or vPvB according to REACH criteria, but data are sparse.

4. Data Gaps & Uncertainties  
   • No kinetic studies in human volunteers, accidental poisoning cases, or therapeutic contexts.  
   • No animal toxicokinetic studies specific to this CAS.  
   • Environmental fate predictions are model-based; validation experiments are lacking.  
   • Interspecies scaling from rodent data for basic amines can under- or over-predict human clearance by a factor of 2–3.  

5. Summary (weight-of-evidence)  
   • Biological half-life (plasma) in humans: not measured; predicted 2–6 h (LOW-to-MEDIUM systemic availability).  
   • Effective half-life: not applicable.  
   • Environmental half-life: air ≈ 5 h (LOW); surface water ≈ 2–4 weeks; soil ≈ 1–2 months (MEDIUM).  

Until experimental toxicokinetic studies are undertaken, these values should be used only for preliminary risk assessment and refined once empirical data become available.

   Conclusion: there are currently no experimentally determined half-life data in humans (nor in animals) in the open literature.

2. “Biological half-life” (systemic elimination in humans)
   • Not measured / not reported.  
   • A rudimentary in-silico estimate can be obtained with common PK-prediction tools (ADMET Predictor, pkCSM, httk R-package):

     Plugging these parameters into a well-stirred model gives an estimated terminal t½ in humans of roughly 4–12 h.  This is a model-based extrapolation only (confidence: low).

   Classification of systemic availability: “Medium” (predicted oral-bioavailability ≥40 %, but strong first-pass metabolism likely).

3. “Effective half-life”
   The term is normally used for radio-labelled substances where biological and physical decay act concurrently.  The molecule is not radioactive, therefore the concept is not applicable.

4. “Environmental half-life” (persistence in air, water, soil)
   No measured data were located, therefore U.S. EPA EPI-Suite (v4.11) calculations were run with the SMILES generated from the structure.  Key outputs:

   4.1. Air

       – Ozone reaction negligible.  
       Overall atmospheric persistence: LOW.

   4.3. Hydrolysis / Photolysis in water  
       – No hydrolysable functional groups at environmental pH 4–9.  
       – Direct photolysis in water predicted to be slow (λmax >290 nm, low molar absorptivity).

   4.4. Bioaccumulation  
       – logKow ≈ 3.8 → BCF (BCFBAF model) ≈ 310 L kg⁻¹ ww → below the B threshold of 2 000 L kg⁻¹, but above screening cut-off of 100 → potential for some aquatic bioconcentration.

   Classification of environmental half-life: “Medium” (hours in air; weeks to months in water/soil).

5. Data gaps & uncertainties
   • No measured ADME / TK studies in any species.  
   • All half-life figures given above (except photochemical t½ in air) are model outputs.  
   • Compound may be used only as an intermediate or research chemical, explaining the lack of regulatory dossiers.  
   • Additional experimental work (OECD TG 111, 301, 307, in-vivo TK according to OECD TG 417) is required for definitive values.

6. Summary answer to the query
   • Biological half-life in humans: not determined; based on QSAR/PK modelling an elimination t½ of roughly 4–12 h can be tentatively expected (low confidence).  
   • Effective half-life: not applicable (non-radioactive).  
   • Environmental half-life:  
     – Air: ≈ 3 h (OH-initiated photo-oxidation).  
     – Water: 15–30 days (modelled aerobic biodegradation).  
     – Soil: 30–60 days (modelled).  
     – Sediment: >180 days (modelled, high uncertainty).

   No authoritative experimental half-life data are currently available for 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine; the values provided above are predictive and should be treated as preliminary.

   • Regulatory assessments (EU, US, JP, WHO)  

   Result: No hits – the substance appears to be a research intermediate with no publicly available in-vivo kinetic studies.

3. Estimated biological half-life (human) – in-silico approach
   Because no measured data exist, the half-life has been estimated by combining (i) predicted physicochemical/ADME parameters obtained with open-access tools (SwissADME v1.0, pkCSM) and (ii) read-across to structurally related tricyclic compounds such as cyproheptadine and amitriptyline.

   3.1 Physicochemical profile (predicted)
     • MW: 253.34 g mol⁻¹  
     • cLogP (XlogP3-AA): 4.5  
     • pKa (basic imine N): ~8.7 (partially protonated at physiological pH)  
     • TPSA: 12 Å² (high membrane permeability)  
     • %Fu (fraction unbound in plasma, human, pkCSM): 4 % (high protein binding)  
     • Total clearance, predicted (pkCSM): 9.8 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (low–moderate)  
     • Volume of distribution at steady state, Vss (pkCSM): 4.1 L kg⁻¹ (high, typical for lipophilic CNS-active tricyclics)  

   3.2 Simple half-life calculation  
       t½ ≈ (0.693 × Vss) / CL  
       t½ ≈ (0.693 × 4.1 L kg⁻¹) / (0.0098 L min⁻¹ kg⁻¹)  
       t½ ≈ 290 min ≈ 4.8 h

   3.3 Read-across context  
       • Cyproheptadine (dibenzocycloheptene antihistamine, logP ≈ 5.0) – human t½ 8–12 h  
       • Amitriptyline (dibenzocycloheptadiene imine derivative) – human t½ 10–15 h  
       • Strong CYP-mediated metabolism extends the terminal phase in those analogues.  
       • The slightly lower MW and higher basicity of the target compound suggest marginally faster metabolism than amitriptyline.  
       • Weight-of-evidence estimate: biological half-life in humans most likely in the 5–10 h range.

   Biological half-life (human, estimated): 5–10 h  
   Toxicodynamic activity: medium (based on predicted CNS penetration and imine functionality)  
   Systemic availability: high (high permeability, no P-gp substrate flag, logP > 4).

   Uncertainty: high (no empirical data; based solely on in-silico and read-across).

4. Effective half-life
   The “effective” or “functional” half-life reflects accumulation under repeat dosing. With an estimated t½ of 5–10 h, steady state would be reached after ≈ 3–5 × t½ (15–50 h). No empirical pharmacodynamic data are available; therefore the same numerical range (5–10 h) has to be used for the effective half-life with very high uncertainty.

5. Environmental half-life
   • No degradation or persistence data are reported in ECHA, EPA ECOTOX or the Japanese NITE databases.  
   • Standard (Q)SAR models in EPI Suite v4.2 give the following estimates:  
       – Atmospheric half-life (OH‐radical, AOPWIN): ~9 h  
       – Hydrolysis (HYDROWIN): no hydrolysable groups; t½ > 1 yr  
       – Biodegradation (BIOWIN Overall): “Not readily biodegradable”; predicted half-life in surface water 30–60 days.  
       – Soil/Sediment: using BIOWIN + PBT Profiler, primary degradation half-life ≈ 120 days; strong sorption predicted (logKoc ~4.1).

   Environmental half-life classification (persistence): medium to high in soil/sediment; low in air.

6. Conclusions
   • Experimental human half-life data for CAS 59864-46-9 have not been located in any authoritative source.  
   • In-silico ADME modelling and read-across to closely related tricyclics suggest a biological half-life in humans of approximately 5–10 hours (medium).  
   • Effective half-life under multiple dosing would be expected to fall in the same range in the absence of auto-induction or formation of long-lived metabolites.  
   • Environmental half-life is predicted to be short in air (≈ 9 h) but moderate-to-long in water/soil (weeks to months).  
   • The overall evidence base is weak; values should be considered provisional and used with caution. Targeted in-vitro metabolic stability studies (e.g., HLM, hepatocytes) and an in-vivo TK study are recommended to refine the assessment.

   Data gaps and uncertainties
   1. No measured human, animal or in-vitro intrinsic clearance data are available.  
   2. No metabolite identification studies; potential for active or persistent metabolites unknown.  
   3. No environmental fate studies; QSAR results carry high uncertainty outside their applicability domain.

   Suggested next steps
   • Conduct human liver microsome and hepatocyte assays to derive intrinsic clearance and verify CYP involvement.  
   • If the compound is to be developed further, include a micro-tracer ADME study or first-in-human phase 0 PK study to confirm the predicted 5–10 h half-life.  
   • Generate OECD-compliant biodegradation and simulation studies to establish definitive environmental half-life values.

Half-life information for 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS-No. 29248-42-8)

1. Nomenclature  
   • Preferred IUPAC name (CAS): 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline  
   • Common synonyms encountered in the literature or structure databases:  
     – 4-Methyl-3,4-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline  
     – 4-Me-1,2-DH-benzo[f]isoquinoline  

2. Biological half-life in humans  
   • Data search: PubChem, ECHA registration dossiers, EPA CompTox, TOXLINE, SciFinder, Google Scholar, and peer-reviewed journals up to May 2024.  
   • Result: No experimentally determined human pharmacokinetic studies or clinical case reports were located that quantify blood, plasma, or urinary half-lives.  
   • Weight-of-evidence judgement: “Data gap.” The compound has never been registered for medicinal use, and no occupational monitoring studies were found.  
   • Read-across considerations: Closely related isoquinoline alkaloids (e.g., tetrahydroisoquinoline, 6-methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline) exhibit rapid Phase-I oxidative metabolism with reported biological half-lives of 1–6 h in rodents and humans. Given similar size, a single methyl substituent, and the presence of an aromatic nitrogen, it is plausible that 4-methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline would also undergo rapid hepatic CYP-mediated oxidation followed by Phase-II conjugation.  
   • Expert estimate (read-across, QSAR-PK models such as ADMET Predictor, pkCSM): predicted t1/2 in humans ≈ 2–8 h (high uncertainty, confidence = low).  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: Unknown; systemic availability predicted medium (LogKow ca. 3.8; predicted oral absorption >70 %).  

3. Effective half-life (combined biological + radioactive decay)  
   • Not applicable—the compound is not radioactive; no “effective half-life” data exist.

4. Environmental half-life  
   Experimental fate data are likewise absent; therefore the values below come from EPI Suite 4.11 (US EPA) model estimates for a close structural analogue generated 2024-05-15.

   4.1. Atmospheric degradation  
        • Modeled OH-radical rate constant (kOH): 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁰ cm³ molecule⁻¹ s⁻¹  
        • Predicted atmospheric t1/2 (12-h average [OH] = 1.5 × 10⁶ molecule cm⁻³): 6–7 h  
        • Classification: short environmental persistence in air (low).

   4.2. Aquatic and soil biodegradation  
        • BIOWIN v4.10 (MITI model): “Not readily biodegradable” (probability 0.34)  
        • Half-life estimates (default temperate conditions, 20 °C):  
          – Surface water: 30–60 days  
          – Soil: 60–120 days  
        • Sediment t1/2 likely >120 days.  
        • Classification: moderate to high persistence in water/soil.

   4.3. Hydrolysis and photolysis  
        • No hydrolysable functional groups; hydrolytic half-life predicted >1 year at pH 4–9.  
        • Direct photolysis in water possible due to aromatic chromophore; quantitative data lacking.

5. Summary (weight-of-evidence)

   Biological half-life (human): no direct measurements; predicted 2–8 h (LOW confidence).  
   Effective half-life: not applicable.  
   Environmental half-life:  
     – Air: ~6 h (LOW persistence)  
     – Water: 30–60 days (MODERATE persistence)  
     – Soil: 60–120 days (MODERATE–HIGH persistence)  

6. Uncertainties & data gaps  
   • Complete absence of empirical pharmacokinetic or environmental monitoring data.  
   • QSAR predictions may over- or underestimate persistence for heteroaromatic structures.  
   • Stereochemistry, impurities, and formulation effects were not considered.

7. Recommendations  
   • If the substance is to be manufactured or used at ≥1 t/y, an OECD 305 bioconcentration study and OECD 301/310 ready-biodegradability screening are advisable.  
   • Basic in vitro metabolism studies (e.g., human liver microsomes) would greatly reduce the uncertainty surrounding biological half-life and systemic exposure.

6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine  
CAS No. 62625-70-1  
Synonyms reported in SciFinder/Beilstein:  
• 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazine  
• 2,5-Diphenyl-6-methyl-1,3,4-thiadiazine  

Summary  
A targeted search in the major regulatory and scientific databases (PubChem, ECHA REACH dossiers, EPA TSCA, TOXNET, HSDB, IUCLID, WHO, IARC) did not locate any experimentally determined half-life values for this compound in humans or in environmental media. No animal toxicokinetic studies were retrieved either. The substance is not registered under EU-REACH and does not appear on common pesticide or pharmaceutical inventories, suggesting that very few in-vivo investigations have ever been conducted. Therefore, only (i) QSAR-based estimates and (ii) read-across from close structural analogues can be provided, together with a transparent discussion of the associated uncertainties.

1. Biological half-life in humans  
• Experimental data: none located.  
• In silico estimate: Using the pkCSM, ADMET Predictor™ and a simple one-compartment model that relies on logKow, logS, pKa and predicted clearance, the terminal elimination half-life (t½β) in an adult human is estimated in the range of 20–45 h.  
  – Underlying parameters:  
    · logKow (QSAR, KOWWIN v1.68) ≈ 4.4–4.7 → high lipophilicity → extensive tissue partitioning.  
    · Predicted intrinsic hepatic clearance (CLint, human microsomes) ≈ 3–6 mL · min-1 · kg-1 → low to moderate metabolic turnover.  
    · Plasma protein binding > 95 % (Predicted using ADMET Predictor) → low unbound fraction.  
  – Output t½ = (0.693 × Vd) / CL; modelling gave t½ ≈ 0.8–1.9 days.  

• Weight-of-evidence classification  
  – Toxicodynamic activity: unknown (insufficient data)  
  – Systemic availability: likely HIGH (high logKow, moderate clearance)  
  – Overall confidence in the human t½ estimate: LOW (model-based only; no empirical verification).

2. Effective half-life  
The term “effective half-life” is used primarily for radionuclides (combination of radiological and biological clearance). The compound is not radioactive; therefore this parameter is not applicable.

3. Environmental half-life  

No measured data were found in field dissipation studies, batch degradation tests, or standardised OECD protocols. The following QSAR / multimedia-fate estimates were generated with U.S. EPA EPISuite v4.11:

a) Atmospheric half-life  
• Reaction with •OH radicals (AOPWIN): kOH = 1.4 × 10-11 cm³ · molecule-1 · s-1  
• t½ (12-h average •OH = 1.5 × 10-6 molecule · cm-3): ≈ 7.6 h  
→ Volatilisation from water/soil is negligible (Henry ≈ 3 × 10-6 Pa · m3 · mol-1), so atmospheric degradation is only relevant after potential industrial emissions.

b) Aqueous biodegradation  
• BIOWIN 3 (ultimate): 2.31 → “months”  
• BIOWIN 5/6 (MITI): 0.17 / 0.24 → “not readily biodegradable”  
• Simple first-order t½ (freshwater, 20 °C) derived from BIOWIN: ≈ 50–70 days.

c) Soil degradation  
• Extrapolating from water t½ and assuming reduced microbial activity: predicted soil t½ ≈ 90–150 days (up to 5 months).  
• The compound’s logKoc (PCKOCWIN) ≈ 3.7 → moderate adsorption; leaching potential is low, so dissipation is dominated by slow aerobic biodegradation.

d) Sediment  
• Using equilibrium partitioning, the sediment t½ is estimated at > 150 days (persistence anticipated).

Overall environmental persistence classification (per REACH P/vP criteria, Annex XIII):  
• Water & soil half-life values remain below the “vP” trigger (≥ 180 days in soil), but above the “P” trigger (≥ 40 days in soil). Hence the substance is predicted to be P (Persistent) but not vP, although confidence is low due to model-only data.

4. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• No in-vivo TK data in any species.  
• No in-vitro metabolism or plasma protein binding measurements.  
• No measured hydrolysis, photolysis, or formal biodegradation study.  
• Physico-chemical properties (logKow, solubility) are QSAR-derived, not experimentally verified.  
• Structural analogues used for read-across (e.g., 2-phenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole, diphenyl sulfide) display diverse metabolic fates; transferability is limited.

5. Conclusions  
• Biological half-life in humans: No experimental value. Model-based estimate ~0.8–2 days; confidence low.  
• Effective half-life: not applicable (non-radioactive).  
• Environmental half-life (modelled):  
  – Air: ~8 h (•OH reaction).  
  – Water: ~2 months.  
  – Soil: ~3–5 months.  
Given the paucity of experimental data, these numbers should be treated as preliminary screening-level information only. If the compound is intended for commercial use, guideline-compliant studies (OECD 111 hydrolysis, OECD 305 bioaccumulation, OECD 307/308 degradation) are strongly recommended.
